built with pdoc

bground.api

Module: bground.api

Definition of API (Application Programming Interface) of the BGROUND package.

  • The API can be used as a simple UI within Spyder or Jupyter.
  • The main purpose of the API: easy access to functions for bkgr subtraction.

Minimal example of running a background subtraction method:

>>> import bground.api as bkg
>>> IN_FILE  = r'input_data.txt'
>>> BKG_FILE = r'processed_data.txt'
>>> bkg.Run.InteractivePlot(IN_FILE, BKG_FILE)

More details to all individual background subtraction methods:

  • bground.api.InteractivePlot = semi-automatatic method, universal, finished
  • bground.api.RestoreFromPoints = special case of the previous, restore bkg from saved bkg points
  • bground.api.SimpleFuncs = automatic methods, fit bkg with a simple funcs, TODO - Edvard (+ Adri)
  • bground.api.BaseLines = automatic method, fit bkg with PyBaseLines funcs
  • bground.api.WaveletMethod = automatic method, fit bkg with wavelet-based funcs, TODO - Adriana
   1'''
   2Module: bground.api
   3-------------------
   4
   5Definition of API (Application Programming Interface) of the BGROUND package.
   6
   7* The API can be used as a simple UI within Spyder or Jupyter.
   8* The main purpose of the API: easy access to functions for bkgr subtraction.
   9
  10Minimal example of running a background subtraction method:
  11    
  12>>> import bground.api as bkg
  13>>> IN_FILE  = r'input_data.txt'
  14>>> BKG_FILE = r'processed_data.txt'
  15>>> bkg.Run.InteractivePlot(IN_FILE, BKG_FILE)
  16
  17More details to all individual background subtraction methods:
  18
  19* bground.api.InteractivePlot
  20  = semi-automatatic method, universal, finished
  21* bground.api.RestoreFromPoints
  22  = special case of the previous, restore bkg from saved bkg points  
  23* bground.api.SimpleFuncs
  24  = automatic methods, fit bkg with a simple funcs, TODO - Edvard (+ Adri)
  25* bground.api.BaseLines
  26  = automatic method, fit bkg with PyBaseLines funcs
  27* bground.api.WaveletMethod
  28  = automatic method, fit bkg with wavelet-based funcs, TODO - Adriana
  29'''
  30
  31
  32# {points} sub-package
  33import bground.points.bdata 
  34import bground.points.bfunc
  35import bground.points.ifunc
  36# sub-packages for fully automated bkg subtraction
  37import bground.sfunc.sfunc
  38import bground.blines.blines
  39
  40# Reading and analyzing input data
  41import numpy as np
  42import pandas as pd
  43from pathlib import Path
  44
  45# Plotting
  46import matplotlib
  47import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
  48
  49# Inteligent dedent (in Help class)
  50import re
  51
  52
  53class InputData:
  54    '''
  55    Class defining {InputData} for background subtraction.
  56    
  57    * Input data can be:
  58      filename, np.array, pd.DataFrame, or ediff.io.Diffractogram1D object.
  59    * In any case, the input data should contain two columns:
  60      `[X, Y = Iraw = raw_intensity]`.
  61    '''
  62    
  63    def __init__(self, input_data, **kwargs):
  64        '''
  65        Initialize {InputData} object that defines input for bkg subtraction.
  66
  67        Parameters
  68        ----------
  69        input_data : str or np.array or pd.DataFrame or ediff.io.Diffraction1D
  70            The input_data are converted to
  71            self.name, self.data and self.diff1D sub-objects.
  72            Self.name = name of the input file (or description of the source).
  73            Self.data = np.array with 2 rows: `[X, Y = Iraw = raw intensity]`.
  74            Self.diff1D = None or ediff.io.Diffraction1D object
  75            if it was given as an argument.
  76        kwargs : keyword arguments for pd.read_csv, optional
  77            The keyword arguments are relevant only if {input_data}
  78            is str/PathLike object defining the input data file.
  79            The keyword arguments are passed to pd.read_csv function
  80            to read the file as a simple DataFrame, which is immediately
  81            converted to a simple np.array = to self.data sub-object
  82            described above.
  83
  84        Returns
  85        -------
  86        bground.api.InputData
  87            The object is fully initialized
  88            and ready to use in background subtraction methods.
  89            It contains the self.name,
  90            self.data and (optionally) self.diff1D sub-objects described above.
  91            
  92        Technical notes
  93        ---------------
  94        * If {input_data} is a file, we read it to np.array with
  95          pd.read_csv instead of np.loadtxt, as it is more flexible/powerful.
  96        * The {kwargs} are pre-set reasonably enough to read most of common
  97          files, but user can adjuste them if needed - of course.
  98        * If {kwargs} need to be adjusted, see the comments in the source
  99          code and pandas.read_csv online documentation.
 100        '''
 101        
 102        # Initialization of InputData object.
 103        # * Two pieces of input:
 104        #   input_data (file or array or DataFrame or ediff.io.Diffraction1D)
 105        #   kwargs (to read the file using pd.read_csv function
 106        # * The input is saved in two/three properties:
 107        #   self.name = name of the input file (or str describing data source)
 108        #   self.data = the XY-data itself = array with 2 rows [X,Y=Intensity]
 109        #   self.diff1D = extra object if ediff.io.Diffracton1D was the input
 110        #                 this is set to None if the input was "common data"
 111        self.name, self.data, self.diff1D = \
 112            InputData.read_input_data(input_data, **kwargs)
 113        
 114    
 115    @staticmethod
 116    def read_input_data(input_data, **kwargs):
 117        '''
 118        Read {input_data} using optional {kwargs}
 119        return the results to be saved in {InputData} object properties.
 120    
 121        * intrinsic method of {InputData} object
 122        * more info => look at the detailed comments in the func source code
 123        '''
 124        # Read input data with XY-data.
 125        # No docstring: just class description above + comments below.
 126        # * The optional arguments (comment, sep, usecols)
 127        #   are passed to pandas.read_csv if the input_data ~ filename.
 128    
 129        if isinstance(input_data, (str, Path)):
 130            # The input is a file = filename given be str or PathLike var.
 131            # => read the file using pd.read_csv
 132            # => user's responsibility: modify optional args as needed
 133            #    the optional arguments are: comment, sep, usecols
 134            #    these arguments are passed to pd.read_csv
 135            # (a) set name = filename
 136            name = str(input_data)
 137            # (b) get **kwargs for pd.read_csv OR set reasonable defaults
 138            # ... comments may be present
 139            my_comment  = kwargs.get('comment') or '#'
 140            # ... header should be None if there are no column headers!
 141            my_header   = kwargs.get('header') or 'infer'
 142            # ... skiprows can be defined for completness and flexibility
 143            my_skiprows = kwargs.get('skiprows') or 0
 144            # ... separator can be defined, defalt is any whitespace
 145            my_sep      = kwargs.get('sep') or r'\s+'
 146            # ... usecols may be defined, default is the first two columns
 147            my_usecols  = kwargs.get('usecols') or [0,1]
 148            # ... columns may be re-defined OR default column names are used
 149            my_columns  = kwargs.get('columns') or None
 150            # (c) read the file with pd.read_csv and the **kwargs from above 
 151            df = pd.read_csv(input_data, 
 152                comment=my_comment, header=my_header, skiprows=my_skiprows, 
 153                sep=my_sep, usecols=my_usecols)
 154            if my_columns is not None: df.columns = my_columns
 155            # (d) convert pd.DataFrame to np.ndarray
 156            #     and transpose the array (columns should be the 1st index)
 157            data = np.transpose(np.array(df))
 158            # (e) set that the data are NOT the special ed.io.Profile
 159            diff1D = None
 160        elif isinstance(input_data, np.ndarray):
 161            # The input is a numpy array.
 162            # => just assign array to data
 163            # => user's responsibility: ensure that the numpy.array
 164            #    contains just two rows: X-values and Y-values
 165            #    (for example, pass something like: arr[[0,2]]
 166            name = 'np.ndarray'
 167            data = input_data
 168            diff1D = None
 169        elif type(input_data) == pd.DataFrame:
 170            # The input is pandas.DataFrame
 171            # => convert DataFrame to numpy.array
 172            # => user's responsibility: ensure that the pandas.Dataframe
 173            #    contains just two columns: X-values and Y-values
 174            #    (for example, pass something like: df[['Pixel','Iraw']]
 175            # * we use type(input_data)==pd.DataFrame instead of isinstance
 176            #   we need to do this due the the next option = {ediff.io.Profile}
 177            #   => {ediff.io.Profile} is-instance of pd.DataFrame
 178            #   => we would think that {Profiles} are DataFrames 
 179            name = 'pd.DataFrame'
 180            data = np.transpose(np.array(input_data))
 181            diff1D = None
 182        elif input_data.__class__.__name__ == 'Diffractogram1D':
 183            # The input is ediff.io.Profile.
 184            # (special case, input from our own, connected package ediff
 185            # => convert Profile to numpy.array
 186            # => the Profile has more-or-less fixed structure
 187            #    the array is created from Profile columns {Pixel} a {Iraw}
 188            # * we use var.__class__.__name__ instead of type
 189            #   we DO NOT KNOW ediff.io.Profile here - it is not imported
 190            #   we could import this, but then bground would depend on ediff
 191            name = 'ediff.io.Diffractogram1D'
 192            data = np.transpose(np.array(input_data[['Pixels','Iraw']]))
 193            diff1D = input_data
 194        else:
 195            raise TypeError('Unknown type of input data!')
 196         
 197        # Return the result = 2xN numpy array with XY-data.
 198        # (i.e. data[0] = X-data/values, data[1] = Y-data/values)
 199        return(name, data, diff1D)
 200
 201
 202class BkgParams:
 203    '''
 204    Class defining {BkgParams} = background parameters
 205    for background subtraction.
 206    
 207    * BkgParams, group 1 = name of the output/background file(s).
 208    * BkgParams, group 2 = x,y-labels and x,y-limits of the interactive plot
 209    * BkgParams, group 3 = messages argument;
 210      if True the program prints brief messages on stdout. 
 211    '''
 212    
 213    def __init__(self, bkg_file=None, 
 214                 xlabel=None, ylabel=None, xlim=None, ylim=None, 
 215                 saveTXT=True, messages=True):
 216        '''
 217        Initialize {BkgParams} object that defines properties of background.
 218
 219        Parameters
 220        ----------
 221        bkg_file : str or PathLike object
 222            Name of the background/output file(s).
 223            There can be three different output files:
 224            data file (XY-data, basic output; {bkg_file}.txt),
 225            background points (if bkg points are defined; {bkg_file}.txt.bp),
 226            background plot (if user requests this; {bkg_file}.txt.png).
 227        xlabel : str, optional, default is None
 228            Label of X-axis (in the background plots).
 229        ylabel : str, optional, default is None
 230            Label of X-axis (in the background plots).
 231        xlim : float or tuple of two floats, optional, default is None
 232            X-range (in the background plots).
 233            If xlim = 300, set Xrange/xlim to (0,300).
 234            If xlim = (50,300), set Xrange/xlim to (50,300).
 235        ylim : float or tuple of two floats, optional, default is None
 236            Y-range (in the background plots).
 237            If ylim = 300, set Yrange/ylim to (0,300).
 238            If ylim = (50,300), set Yrange/ylim to (50,300).
 239        saveTXT : bool, optional, default is True
 240            If True (default), save also in TXT and TXT.BP files.
 241            If False, the results are saved only within this object.
 242            The False in suitable for certain automated processing paths.            
 243        messages : bool, optional, default is True
 244            If True, the program shows short messages on stdout as it runs.
 245
 246        Returns
 247        -------
 248        bground.api.BkgParams object
 249            The object is fully initialized and ready to be used.
 250            In fact the object is simple
 251            - just a collectoin of properties, no methods.
 252        '''
 253        # Initialization of BkgParams object => 3 groups of params:
 254        #  1) self.bkg_file = name of background file(s)
 255        #  2) self.xlabel/ylabel/xlim/ylim = params of bkg plots
 256        #  3) self.saveTXT  = if True (default), save results in TXT-files
 257        #     self.messages = if True (default), print short messages on stdout
 258        #------
 259        
 260        # (1) Set the name of background/output file(s), which can be:
 261        # * some.txt     = main output, 4 cols = [X, Iraw, Ibkg, I=(Iraw-Ibkg)]
 262        # * some.txt.bp  = saved background points (for possible recalculation)
 263        # * some.txt.png = saved plot (from interactive matplotlib interface)
 264        #------
 265        if bkg_file is None:
 266            # In some cases, we may not want to save output to TXT-files.
 267            # => then we save the data just in the calling object:
 268            # => specifically in: self.data, self.background, and self.diff1D.
 269            self.bkg_file = None
 270        else:
 271            # In classical usage, we save the data also in output files, 
 272            # If {bkg_file} is a PathLike object, convert to string
 273            bkg_file = str(bkg_file)
 274            # If {bkg_file} ends with '.bp' or '.txt', remove the extension
 275            # (this may be a copy paste error
 276            # (OR intentional in the inherited method RestoreFromBackground,
 277            # (where the input bkg_file usually HAS the extension '.txt.bp'
 278            bkg_file = bkg_file.lower()
 279            bkg_file = bkg_file.removesuffix('.bp')
 280            bkg_file = bkg_file.removesuffix('.png')
 281            # Ii the out_file name does not have .txt extension, add
 282            if not(bkg_file.lower().endswith('.txt')):
 283                bkg_file = bkg_file + '.txt'
 284            # Save the final name to self.out_file
 285            self.bkg_file = bkg_file
 286        
 287        # (2) Set the interactive plot parameters
 288        # (x,y-labels
 289        self.xlabel = xlabel            # x-axis label of the interactive plot
 290        self.ylabel = ylabel            # y-axis label of the interactive plot
 291        # (x,y-limits
 292        if isinstance(xlim,list): self.xlim = xlim  # xlim = [xmin,xmax]
 293        else: self.xlim = [0,xlim]                  # ...or just xmax
 294        if isinstance(ylim,list): self.ylim = ylim  # ylim = [ymin,ymax]
 295        else: self.ylim = [0,ylim]                  # ...or just ymax
 296        
 297        # (3) Additional parameters
 298        # saveTXT - if {True} save results also TXT and TXT.BP files
 299        # messages - if {True} print brief messages to stdout
 300        self.saveTXT = saveTXT
 301        self.messages = messages
 302        
 303
 304class InteractivePlot:
 305    '''
 306    Class defining {InteractivePlot} method of backround subtraction.
 307    
 308    * When running the method, a new window with interactive plot is opened.
 309    * The user defines background points using mouse and keyboard shortcuts.
 310    * The program does the rest - it subtracts bkg + shows/saves the results.
 311    
 312    The method can be run in two ways:
 313        
 314    * Classical, step-by-step approach - see the example below
 315    * Modern, single-funtion approach - see bground.api.Run.InteractivePlot
 316    
 317    Example - running InteractivePlot in classical way:
 318        
 319    >>> # Standard import
 320    >>> # (alternative: import ediff as ed => bkg is available as ed.bkg
 321    >>> import bground.api as bkg
 322    >>>
 323    >>> # Define I/O files
 324    >>> # (input file  = XYdata, 2 cols: [X, Y = Iraw = Raw Intensity]
 325    >>> # (output file = XYdata, 4 cols: [X, Iraw, Inet, I = (Iraw - Inet)]
 326    >>> IN  = 'data1_raw_intensity.txt'
 327    >>> OUT = 'data2_bkg_subtracted.txt'
 328    >>>
 329    >>> # Define auxiliary objects
 330    >>> DATA = bkg.InputData(IN, usecols=[0,1], unpack=True)
 331    >>> PPAR = bkg.BkgParams(OUT,'Pix','Intensity',xlim=[0,200],ylim=[0,180])
 332    >>>
 333    >>> # Initialize and run InteractivePlot subtraction method
 334    >>> # (the method is initialized using the auxiliary objects and run
 335    >>> # (SMET.data + SMET.background sub-objects will contain the results
 336    >>> SMET = bkg.InteractivePlot(DATA, PPAR)
 337    >>> SMET.run()
 338    '''
 339    
 340    
 341    def __init__(self, DATA, PARS, CLI=False):
 342        '''
 343        Initialize {InteractivePlot} object.
 344
 345        Parameters
 346        ----------
 347        DATA : bground.api.InputData object
 348            The object contains description of input data.
 349            The {InputData} object should be defined
 350            either before initializing the {InteractivePlot},
 351            or intrinsically if we use bground.api.Run.InteractivePlot.
 352        PARS : bground.api.BkgParams object
 353            The object contains description of input data.
 354            The {BkgData} object should be defined
 355            either before initializing the {InteractivePlot},
 356            or intrinsically if we use bground.api.Run.InteractivePlot.
 357        CLI : bool, optional, default is False
 358            If we run the {InteractivePLot} from CLI interface,
 359            this arbument should be set to True so that the plot stayed
 360            in the screen.
 361
 362        Returns
 363        -------
 364        bground.api.InteractivePlot object
 365            The object should be ready to use.
 366            The principal object method is InteractivePlot.run.
 367        '''
 368        
 369        # Save properties from DATA object
 370        self.name = DATA.name
 371        self.data = DATA.data
 372        self.diff1D = DATA.diff1D
 373        
 374        # Save properties from PARS object
 375        self.pars = PARS
 376        
 377        # Additional property - empty XYbackground object
 378        # (this object is defined as a semi-empty object here
 379        # (the only argument we supply is the name of the background file
 380        self.background = \
 381            bground.points.bdata.XYbackground(self.pars.bkg_file)
 382            
 383        # Initialize plotting - by means of composition
 384        self.plots = Plots(self)
 385        
 386        # Initialize specific interactive backend
 387        # in case Python runs in CLI = Command Line Interface,
 388        # i.e. if the program runs outside Spyder or Jupyter environments
 389        if CLI == True:
 390            matplotlib.use('QtAgg')
 391
 392        
 393    def run(self):
 394        '''
 395        Run {InteractivePlot} method of background subtraction.
 396        
 397        * The method (i) clears variables
 398          and (ii) shows the plot on the screen.
 399        * The plot is waiting for the user to define
 400          background points with keyboard and mouse. 
 401        * A brief help is printed on stdout
 402          when the interactive plot is opened.
 403        * No params; everything defined during InteractivePlot initialization.
 404        '''
 405
 406        # (0) Clear background points from possible previous runs.
 407        # * Issue in Jupyter, when re-running the cell with this command
 408        self.background.points.X = []
 409        self.background.points.Y = []
 410        
 411        # (1) Run the interactive plot
 412        # * the plot appears in a new window
 413        #   if we use the recommended %matplotlib qt
 414        # * in addition, a brief help is printed to to stdout
 415        #   = Screen in Python, Console in Spyder, output cell in Jupyter
 416        # * the return values seem to be necessary for current Jupyter version
 417        fig,ax = bground.points.ifunc.interactive_plot(self)
 418        
 419        # (2) Optimize the layout of the returned figure and show the plot.
 420        # * fig.tight_layout() functin works
 421        #   in all three supported interfaces: CLI, Spyder, Jupyter
 422        fig.tight_layout()
 423        plt.show()
 424    
 425
 426class RestoreFromPoints(InteractivePlot):
 427    '''
 428    RestoreFromPoints background subtraction method.
 429    
 430    * This is a specific case (and subclass) of InteractivePlot method.
 431    * It inherits the initialization and visualization from InteractivePlot.
 432    * But it runs non-interactively, just reading bkgpoints + calculating bkg.
 433    
 434    The method can be run in two ways:
 435        
 436    * Classical, step-by-step approach - see the example below
 437    * Modern, single-funtion approach - see bground.api.Run.RestoreFromPoints
 438    
 439    Example - running RestoreFromPoints in classical way:
 440        
 441    >>> # Standard import
 442    >>> # (alternative: import ediff as ed => bkg is available as ed.bkg
 443    >>> import bground.api as bkg
 444    >>>
 445    >>> # Define I/O files
 446    >>> IN  = 'data1_raw_intensity.txt'
 447    >>> BKG = 'data2_bkg_subtracted.txt.bp'
 448    >>>
 449    >>> # Define auxiliary objects
 450    >>> DATA = bkg.InputData(IN, usecols=[0,1], unpack=True)
 451    >>> PPAR = bkg.BkgParams(BKG,'Pix','Intensity',xlim=[0,200],ylim=[0,180])
 452    >>>
 453    >>> # Initialize and run RestoreFromPoints subtraction method
 454    >>> # (SMET.data + SMET.background sub-objects will contain the results
 455    >>> SMET = bkg.RestoreFromPoints(DATA, PPAR)
 456    >>> SMET.run()
 457    '''
 458    
 459    def __init__(self, DATA, PARS, CLI=False):
 460        '''
 461        Initialize {RestoreFromPoints} object.
 462
 463        Parameters
 464        ----------
 465        DATA : bground.api.InputData object
 466            The object contains description of input data.
 467            The {InputData} object should be defined
 468            either before initializing the {InteractivePlot},
 469            or intrinsically if we use bground.api.Run.InteractivePlot.
 470        PARS : bground.api.BkgParams object
 471            The object contains description of input data.
 472            The {BkgData} object should be defined
 473            either before initializing the {InteractivePlot},
 474            or intrinsically if we use bground.api.Run.InteractivePlot.
 475        CLI : bool, optional, default is False
 476            If we run the {InteractivePLot} from CLI interface,
 477            this arbument should be set to True so that the plot stayed
 478            in the screen.
 479
 480        Returns
 481        -------
 482        bground.api.RestoreFromPoints object
 483            The object should be ready to use.
 484            The principal object method is InteractivePlot.run.
 485        '''
 486        
 487        # The same initialization like in the superclass.
 488        # (if __init__ contains just this command, it can be ommited
 489        # (here: just for historical reasons; we used to have additional args
 490        super().__init__(DATA, PARS, CLI)
 491        
 492        # Note: it is possible to initialize this function
 493        # with all possible names: 'some', 'some.txt', 'some.txt.bp'
 494        # and the initialization procedure of BkgParams calls resolves this.
 495        
 496        # Note: CLI=True if program runs from pure Python in command line
 497        # => in super().__init__ we set the same matplotlib backend
 498        # => we get the same interface in InteractivePlot and RestoreFromPoints
 499        # => for compatibility with methods show_data_with_bkg_definition ...
 500 
 501
 502    def run(self):
 503        '''
 504        Run {RestoreFromPoints} method of background subtraction.
 505        
 506        * The method reads a TXT.BP file with background points
 507          and recalculates background and saves the results.
 508        * The TXT.BP file usually comes from
 509          a previous run of InteractivePlot method.
 510        * No params; everything defined during InteractivePlot initialization.
 511        '''
 512
 513        # (0) Read background points
 514        # (self.background comes from initialization
 515        # (it contains the name of the file with bkg points
 516        # (this filename is in PARS and saved in self.background.bname
 517        self.background = bground.points.bfunc.load_bkg_points(self)
 518        
 519        # (1) Calculate background-corrected data AND save them to self.data
 520        # (self.data = the RestoreFromPoints object ALWAYS contains the output
 521        self.data = bground.points.bfunc.calculate_bkg_data(self)
 522        
 523        # (2) Save the calculated data ALSO in self.diff1D => if it was defined
 524        # (self.diff1D IF ediff.io.Diffractogram1D used for the initialization 
 525        if self.diff1D is not None:
 526            self.diff1D['Ibkg'] = self.data[2]
 527            self.diff1D['I']    = self.data[3]
 528
 529        # (3) Save the calculated data ALSO to out_file => if it was defined
 530        # (save data to file IF {saveTXT} is True
 531        if self.pars.saveTXT is True:
 532            bground.points.bfunc.save_bkg_data(self)
 533       
 534
 535class SimpleFuncs:
 536    '''
 537    SimpleFunc background subtraction method(s).
 538    
 539    * This is a specific case (and subclass) of InteractivePlot method.
 540    * It inherits the initialization and visualization from InteractivePlot.
 541    * But it runs non-interactively, just reading bkgpoints + calculating bkg.
 542    
 543    The method can be run in two ways:
 544        
 545    * Classical, step-by-step approach - see the example below
 546    * Modern, single-funtion approach - see bground.api.Run.SimpleFunc
 547    
 548    Example - running SimpleFunc in classical way:
 549        
 550    >>> # Standard import
 551    >>> # (alternative: import ediff as ed => bkg is available as ed.bkg
 552    >>> import bground.api as bkg
 553    >>>
 554    >>> # Define I/O files
 555    >>> IN  = 'data1_raw_intensity.txt'
 556    >>> BKG = 'data2_bkg_corrected.txt'
 557    >>>
 558    >>> # Define auxiliary objects
 559    >>> DATA = bkg.InputData(IN, usecols=[0,1], unpack=True)
 560    >>> BPAR = bkg.BkgParams(BKG,'Pixel','Intensity',xlim=[0,200],ylim=[0,280])
 561    >>>
 562    >>> # Initialize and run SimpleFunc bkg subtraction method
 563    >>> # here: selected SimpleFunc method = RollingBall (+ its params)
 564    >>> SMET = bkg.SimpleFuncs(DATA, PPAR)
 565    >>> SMET.run(algorithm='RollingBall', xrange=(40,250), radius=70)
 566
 567    TODO: Evard ...
 568    
 569    * Add simple tailored NumPy/SciPy based function
 570    * This class should not be modified => add func to sfunc.sfunc
 571    '''
 572    
 573    def __init__(self, DATA, PARS):
 574        '''
 575        Initialize {SimpleFunc} object.
 576        
 577        * The {SimpleFunc} object can run simple bkg subtraction methods.
 578
 579        Parameters
 580        ----------
 581        DATA : bground.api.InputData object
 582            The object contains description of input data.
 583            The {InputData} object should be defined
 584            either before initializing the {InteractivePlot},
 585            or intrinsically if we use bground.api.Run.InteractivePlot.
 586        PARS : bground.api.BkgParams object
 587            The object contains description of input data.
 588            The {BkgData} object should be defined
 589            either before initializing the {InteractivePlot},
 590            or intrinsically if we use bground.api.Run.InteractivePlot.
 591
 592        Returns
 593        -------
 594        bground.api.SimpleFunc object
 595            The object should be ready to use.
 596            The principal object method is SimpleFunc.run(method=...).
 597        '''
 598        
 599        # Save properties from DATA object
 600        self.name = DATA.name
 601        self.data = DATA.data
 602        self.diff1D = DATA.diff1D
 603        
 604        # Save properties from PARS object
 605        self.pars = PARS
 606        
 607        # Additional property - empty XYbackground object
 608        # (this object is defined as a semi-empty object here
 609        # (the only argument we supply is the name of the background file
 610        self.background = \
 611            bground.points.bdata.XYbackground(self.pars.bkg_file)
 612                        
 613        # Initialize plotting - by means of composition
 614        self.plots = Plots(self)
 615        
 616
 617    def run(self, algorithm='RollingBall', **kwargs):
 618        '''
 619        Run specific background subtraction {method} from {SimpleFunc} class.
 620        
 621        * Default SimpleFunc method is 'RollingBall'.
 622        * Optional {kwargs} are passed to the selected method.
 623        
 624        Parameters
 625        ----------
 626        method : str, optional, default is 'RollingBall'
 627            Name of the background subtraction method.
 628            Implemented methods: 'RollingBall'.
 629        kwargs : keyword arguments
 630            Arbitrary keyword arguments.
 631            The arguments are passed to selected bkg subtraction {method}.
 632            Example: for 'RollingBall' method, we should specify {radius} arg.
 633        
 634        Summary of kwargs
 635        -----------------
 636        xrange : tuple/list of two floats
 637            Argument relevant to all functions/algorithms.
 638            If None, then the func/algorihtm will use the whole range
 639            as defined in calling object = self.pars.xlim.
 640            If specified (recommended), then the algorithm/func will use
 641            the selected sub-range where are the relevant diffraction peaks.
 642        radius: integer
 643            Argument relevant to 'RollingBall' algorithm.
 644            Radius of the rolling ball.
 645        
 646        Returns
 647        -------
 648        None
 649            The results are saved in self object,
 650            specifically in self.background (bkg curve)
 651            and self.data (bgk-corrected data).
 652            The bkg-corrected data are also saved in a TXT-file
 653            if saveTXT=False argument was not used in initialization.
 654        '''
 655
 656        # (1) Run the selected bkg subtraction method
 657        # (the results are ALWAYS auto-saved in self.background and self.data
 658        if algorithm == 'RollingBall':
 659            bground.sfunc.sfunc.rolling_ball(self, **kwargs)
 660        else:
 661            raise ValueError('Uknown background subtraction method!')
 662        
 663        # (2) Save the calculated data ALSO in self.diff1D => if it was defined
 664        # (self.diff1D IF ediff.io.Diffractogram1D used for the initialization 
 665        if self.diff1D is not None:
 666            self.diff1D['Ibkg'] = self.data[2]
 667            self.diff1D['I']    = self.data[3]
 668
 669        # (3) Save the calculated data ALSO to out_file => if it was defined
 670        # (save data to file IF {saveTXT} is True
 671        # (we can use the existing func from bground.points sub-package
 672        if self.pars.saveTXT is True:
 673            bground.points.bfunc.save_bkg_data(self)
 674        
 675        # (4) Print a brief message if requested
 676        if self.pars.messages is True:
 677            print('Backgroud subtraction :: SimpleFunc/RollingBall.')
 678            if self.pars.saveTXT is True:
 679                print(f'Background file: {self.pars.bkg_file}')
 680
 681class BaseLines:
 682    '''
 683    BaseLines method of backround subtraction.
 684
 685    This method uses algorithms from pybaselines for automatic baseline 
 686    detection.
 687
 688    Example 1 :: algorithm with default parameters
 689
 690    >>> # Standard import
 691    >>> import bground.api as bkg
 692    >>> 
 693    >>> # (1) Define input and output file
 694    >>> IN  = 'ed1_raw.txt'  # input file,  2cols: X, Yraw
 695    >>> OUT = 'ed2_bkg.txt'  # output file, 4cols: X, Yraw, Ybkg, Y=Yraw-Ybkg
 696    >>>
 697    >>> # (2) Call the method, subtract background, and save results.
 698    >>> BMET = bkg.BaseLines(IN, OUT)
 699    >>> BMET.run(algorithm='peak_filling', xrange=(30,250))
 700
 701
 702    Example 2 :: specify algorithm parameters
 703
 704    >>> # Standard import
 705    >>> import bground.api as bkg
 706    >>> 
 707    >>> # (1) Define input and output file
 708    >>> IN  = 'ed1_raw.txt'  # input file,  2cols: X, Yraw
 709    >>> OUT = 'ed2_bkg.txt'  # output file, 4cols: X, Yraw, Ybkg, Y=Yraw-Ybkg
 710    >>>
 711    >>> # (2) Call the method, subtract background, and save results.
 712    >>> # Add parameter decreasing=True for the snip algorithm.
 713    >>> BMET = bkg.BaseLines(IN, OUT)
 714    >>> BMET.run(algorithm='snip', xrange=(30,250), decreasing=True)
 715
 716    '''
 717
 718    
 719    def __init__(self, input_data, bkg_params):
 720        '''
 721        Initialize BaseLines.
 722
 723        Parameters
 724        ----------
 725        input_data : str or bground.api.InputData
 726            Can be InputData directly or any type supported by InputData.
 727        bkg_params : str or bground.api.BkgParams
 728            Can be a string with a output path or BkgParams object.
 729        '''
 730        if isinstance(input_data, InputData):
 731            self.data = input_data.data
 732            self.diff1D = input_data.diff1D
 733        else:
 734            input_data = InputData(input_data)
 735            self.data = input_data.data
 736            self.diff1D = input_data.diff1D
 737
 738        if isinstance(bkg_params, BkgParams):
 739            self.pars = bkg_params
 740        else:
 741            self.pars = BkgParams(bkg_params)
 742
 743        self.x, self.y = self.data
 744        
 745        # necessary for save_bkg_data function
 746        self.background = \
 747            bground.points.bdata.XYbackground(self.pars.bkg_file)
 748
 749        self.plots = Plots(self)
 750
 751    def run(self, algorithm = "peak_filling", xrange=None, **kwargs):
 752        '''Run the background subtraction.
 753
 754        Parameters
 755        ----------
 756        algorithm : str, optional, by default "peak_filling"
 757            Algorithm from pybaselines for baseline detection.     
 758        xrange : tuple with two floats, optional, by default None
 759            A range on the x axis, where the baseline should be subtracted.
 760            The range is inclusive. If None, the whole range is used.
 761
 762        Returns
 763        -------
 764        None
 765            The results are saved in self object,
 766            specifically in self.background (bkg curve)
 767            and self.data (bgk-corrected data).
 768            The bkg-corrected data are also saved in a TXT-file
 769            if saveTXT=False argument was not used in initialization.
 770
 771        Notes
 772        -----
 773        * `kwargs` are passed to the algorithm.
 774
 775        * Recommended algorithms (and parameters in kwargs):
 776            * `peak_filling` (with parameter `half_window=1`),
 777            * `snip` (with parameter `decreasing=True`),
 778            * `pspline_iasls` (with parameter `lam=5`), 
 779            * `irsqr` (with parameter `lam=1000`),
 780            * `rubberband` (with parameter `lam=2`)
 781        
 782        * Please refer to 
 783        https://pybaselines.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api/Baseline.html
 784        for more algorithms and their details.
 785        '''
 786
 787        bline_x, bline_y = bground.blines.blines.calculate_baseline(
 788            self.x, self.y, algorithm, xrange, **kwargs)
 789        
 790        self.background.curve.X, self.background.curve.Y = bline_x, bline_y
 791        self.data = bground.blines.blines.subtract_baseline(
 792            self.x, self.y, bline_y, xrange)
 793        
 794        if self.diff1D is not None:
 795            self.diff1D['Ibkg'] = self.data[2]
 796            self.diff1D['I']    = self.data[3]
 797        
 798        if self.pars.saveTXT:
 799            bground.points.bfunc.save_bkg_data(self)
 800
 801        if self.pars.messages is True:
 802            print(f'Backgroud subtraction :: BaseLines/{algorithm}.')
 803            if self.pars.saveTXT is True:
 804                print(f'Background file: {self.pars.bkg_file}')
 805
 806    
 807
 808class Wavelets:
 809    '''
 810    TODO: Adri ...
 811    
 812    * Implement a simple wavelet method - template: SimpleFuncs - RollingBall
 813    * Then implement additional method(s) according to {Cotret_2017.pdf}
 814    '''        
 815    
 816
 817class Run:
 818    '''
 819    The class with funcs to run each bkg subtraction method in one step.
 820    
 821    * Funcs in this class initialize and run
 822      a selected method in one step.
 823    * They set bground.api.InputData and bground.api.BkgParams
 824      and run the method.
 825    * All parameters are set in one place,
 826      which is convenient for the modern/simple OO-processing.
 827    
 828    List of the available methods:
 829    
 830    * Run.InteractivePlot   = run {InteractivePlot} bkg subtraction method
 831    * Run.RestoreFromPoints = run {RestoreFromPoints} bkg subtration method
 832    * Run.SimpleFuncs       = run {SimpleFuncs} bkg subtraction method(s)
 833    * Run.BaseLines         = run {BaseLines} bkg subtraction method(s)
 834    * Run.Wavelets          = run {Wavelets} bkg subtraction method(s)    
 835    '''
 836
 837    def InteractivePlot(
 838            in_data, bkg_file=None,
 839            comment='#', skiprows=0, header='infer', sep=r'\s+', usecols=[0,1], 
 840            xlabel=None, ylabel=None, xlim=None, ylim=None, 
 841            saveTXT=True, messages=True, CLI=False):
 842        '''
 843        Run bground.api.InteractivePlot method with a single function/command.
 844               
 845        Parameters
 846        ----------
 847        in_data : filename or np.ndarray or pd.DataFrame or ELD profile
 848            Input XYdata, containing 2 columns:
 849            `[X, Y = Iraw = raw intensity]`.
 850        bkg_file : filename, optional, default is None
 851            Name of the background/output file(s).
 852            There can be three different output files:
 853            data file ({bkg_file}.txt),
 854            background points ({bkg_file}.txt.bp),
 855            background plot ({bkg_file}.txt.png).
 856            If {bkg_file} is not specified (None) and {saveTXT}=False,
 857            then the TXT-files are not created and the results are
 858            saved only within the InteractivePlot object
 859            (in self.data, self.background, and possibly self.diff1D).
 860            The self.diff1D sub-object is saved only if the input is
 861            a ediff.io.Diffractogram1D object.
 862        comment, skiprows, header, sep, usecols: params for pd.read_csv func
 863             Parameters that are passed to pd.read_csv function
 864             if the {in_data} is an XYfile with two columns.
 865             See bground.api.InputData docs for more details.
 866         xlabel, ylabel, xlim, ylim: params for plotting
 867             Parameters that are used when plotting the XYdata
 868             in the form of matplotlib interactive graph.
 869             See bground.api.BkgParams docs for more details.
 870        saveTXT : bool, optional, default is True
 871            If True (default), save results in both TXT-file and self.diff1D.
 872            It can be switched to False in certain automated processing paths.            
 873        messages : bool, optional, default is True
 874            If True (default), print short messages on stdout.
 875        CLI : bool, optional, default is False
 876            Must be set to True when program runs from pure Python in CLI.
 877            It ensures that the interactive plot will not close immediately.
 878        
 879        Returns
 880        -------
 881        bground.api.InteractivePlot
 882            The object is used to run the InteractivePlot method,
 883            but it also saves and plots the original and bkg-corrected data.
 884        
 885        Example
 886        -------
 887        
 888        >>> # Standard import
 889        >>> # (alternative: import ediff as ed => bkg is available as ed.bkg
 890        >>> import bground.api as bkg
 891        >>>
 892        >>> # Define I/O files
 893        >>> # (input file  = XYdata, 2 cols: [X, Y = Iraw = Raw Intensity]
 894        >>> # (output file = XYdata, 4 cols: [X, Iraw, Inet, I = (Iraw - Inet)]
 895        >>> IN  = 'data1_raw_intensity.txt'
 896        >>> OUT = 'data2_bkg_subtracted.txt'
 897        >>>
 898        >>> # Run InteractivePlot subtraction method with a single function
 899        >>> # (the func initializes all objects and runs the method
 900        >>> # (SMET.data + SMET.background sub-objects will contain the results
 901        >>> SMET = bkg.Run.InteractivePlot(IN, OUT, 
 902        >>>     xlabel='Pixels', ylabel='Intensity', xlim=300, ylim=300)
 903        '''
 904        
 905        # (1) Define objects with input and output data
 906        DATA = InputData(in_data, sep=sep, usecols=usecols,
 907            comment=comment, skiprows=skiprows, header=header)
 908        PARS = BkgParams(bkg_file, 
 909            xlabel=xlabel, ylabel=ylabel, xlim=xlim, ylim=ylim,
 910            messages=messages, saveTXT=saveTXT)
 911        
 912        # (2) Define the method
 913        # (including optional argument CLI if it runs from pure CLI python
 914        SMET = InteractivePlot(DATA, PARS, CLI=CLI)
 915        
 916        # (3) Run the method
 917        SMET.run()
 918        
 919        # (4) Return the final InteractivePlot object
 920        # (the data are auto-saved to bkg_file(s)
 921        # (BUT returning the object is useful to see/show/plot the data
 922        return(SMET)
 923        
 924    
 925    def RestoreFromPoints(in_data, bkg_file, saveTXT=True,
 926            comment='#', skiprows=0, header='infer', sep=r'\s+', usecols=[0,1], 
 927            xlabel=None, ylabel=None, xlim=None, ylim=None, messages=False):
 928        '''
 929        Run bground.api.RestoreFromPoints method with a single func/command.
 930
 931        Parameters
 932        ----------
 933        in_data : filename or np.ndarray or pd.DataFrame or ELD profile
 934            Input XYdata, containing 2 columns:
 935            `[X, Y = Iraw = raw intensity]`.
 936        bkg_file : filename
 937            Name of the file with background points.
 938            According to bground package convention,
 939            the filename should be `something.txt` or `something.txt.bp`.
 940            TXT-files contain the XY-data
 941            and TXT.BP-files contain background points.
 942            If {bkg_file} is a TXT file, the extension is converted to TXT.BP.
 943        saveTXT : bool, optional, default is True
 944            If True (default), save results in both TXT-file and self.diff1D.
 945            It can be switched to False in certain automated processing paths.            
 946        comment, skiprows, header, sep, usecols: params for pd.read_csv func
 947            Parameters that are passed to pd.read_csv function
 948            if the {in_data} is an XYfile with two columns.
 949            See bground.api.InputData docs for more details.
 950         xlabel, ylabel, xlim, ylim, messages, CLI: params for plotting
 951            Parameters that are used when plotting the XYdata
 952            in the form of matplotlib interactive graph.
 953            See bground.api.BkgParams docs for more details.
 954        
 955        Returns
 956        -------
 957        bground.api.RestoreFromPoints
 958            The object is used to run the RestoreFromPoints method,
 959            but it also saves and plots the original and bkg-corrected data.
 960        
 961        Example
 962        -------
 963        
 964        >>> # Standard import
 965        >>> # (alternative: import ediff as ed => bkg is available as ed.bkg
 966        >>> import bground.api as bkg
 967        >>> 
 968        >>> # Define I/O files
 969        >>> IN  = r'orig_data.txt'
 970        >>> BKG = r'bkg-corrected_data.txt'
 971        >>> 
 972        >>> # Run RestoreFromPoints using a single command
 973        >>> SMET = bkg.Run.RestoreFromPoints(IN, BKG,
 974        >>>         xlabel='Pix', ylabel='Intensity',xlim=[0,200],ylim=180)
 975        '''
 976        
 977        # (1) Define objects with input and output data
 978        DATA = InputData(in_data, sep=sep, usecols=usecols,
 979            comment=comment, skiprows=skiprows, header=header)
 980        PARS = BkgParams(bkg_file, 
 981             xlabel=xlabel, ylabel=ylabel, xlim=xlim, ylim=ylim,
 982             saveTXT=saveTXT, messages=messages)
 983        
 984        # (2) Define the method
 985        # (including optional {out_file} to specify 
 986        SMET = RestoreFromPoints(DATA, PARS, CLI=False)
 987        
 988        # (3) Run the method
 989        SMET.run()
 990        
 991        # (4) Return the final RestoreFromPoints object
 992        # (the data are auto-saved to bkg_file(s)
 993        # (BUT returning the object is useful to see/show/plot the data
 994        return(SMET)
 995    
 996    
 997    def SimpleFuncs(in_data, bkg_file=None, saveTXT=True,
 998            comment='#', skiprows=0, header='infer', sep=r'\s+', usecols=[0,1], 
 999            xlabel=None, ylabel=None, xlim=None, ylim=None, messages=False,
1000            algorithm='RollingBall', **kwargs):
1001        '''
1002        Run bground.api.RestoreFromPoints method with a single func/command.
1003        
1004        TODO: docs => Adri
1005        '''
1006        
1007        # (1) Define objects with input and output data
1008        DATA = InputData(in_data, sep=sep, usecols=usecols,
1009            comment=comment, skiprows=skiprows, header=header)
1010        PARS = BkgParams(bkg_file, 
1011             xlabel=xlabel, ylabel=ylabel, xlim=xlim, ylim=ylim,
1012             saveTXT=saveTXT, messages=messages)
1013        
1014        # (2) Define the method
1015        # (including optional {out_file} to specify
1016        SMET = SimpleFuncs(DATA, PARS)
1017        
1018        # (3) Run the method
1019        SMET.run(algorithm, **kwargs)
1020        
1021        # (4) Return the final RestoreFromPoints object
1022        # (the data are auto-saved to bkg_file(s)
1023        # (BUT returning the object is useful to see/show/plot the data
1024        return(SMET)
1025
1026
1027    def BaseLines(in_data, bkg_file=None, saveTXT=True,
1028            comment='#', skiprows=0, header='infer', sep=r'\s+', usecols=[0,1], 
1029            xlabel=None, ylabel=None, xlim=None, ylim=None, messages=False,
1030            algorithm="peak_filling", xrange=None, **kwargs):
1031        '''
1032        Run bground.api.BaseLines method with a single function/command.
1033
1034        Parameters
1035        ----------
1036        in_data : filename or np.ndarray or pd.DataFrame or ELD profile
1037            Input XYdata, containing 2 columns:
1038            `[X, Y = Iraw = raw intensity]`.
1039        bkg_file : filename
1040            Name of the file with background points.
1041            According to bground package convention,
1042            the filename should be `something.txt` or `something.txt.bp`.
1043            TXT-files contain the XY-data
1044            and TXT.BP-files contain background points.
1045            If {bkg_file} is a TXT file, the extension is converted to TXT.BP.
1046        saveTXT : bool, optional, default is True
1047            If True (default), save results in both TXT-file and self.diff1D.
1048            It can be switched to False in certain automated processing paths.            
1049        comment, skiprows, header, sep, usecols: params for pd.read_csv func
1050            Parameters that are passed to pd.read_csv function
1051            if the {in_data} is an XYfile with two columns.
1052            See bground.api.InputData docs for more details.
1053         xlabel, ylabel, xlim, ylim, messages, CLI: params for plotting
1054            Parameters that are used when plotting the XYdata
1055            in the form of matplotlib interactive graph.
1056            See bground.api.BkgParams docs for more details.
1057        algorithm : str, optional, by default "peak_filling"
1058            Algorithm from pybaselines for baseline detection.        
1059        xrange : tuple with two floats, optional, by default None
1060            A range on the x axis, where the baseline should be subtracted.
1061            The range is inclusive. If None, the whole range is used.
1062
1063        Returns
1064        -------
1065        bground.api.BaseLines
1066            The object is used to run the BaseLines method,
1067            but it also saves and plots the original and bkg-corrected data.
1068
1069        Notes
1070        -----
1071        * `kwargs` are passed to the algorithm.
1072
1073        * Recommended algorithms (and parameters in kwargs):
1074            * `peak_filling` (with parameter `half_window=1`),
1075            * `snip` (with parameter `decreasing=True`),
1076            * `pspline_iasls` (with parameter `lam=5`), 
1077            * `irsqr` (with parameter `lam=1000`),
1078            * `rubberband` (with parameter `lam=2`)
1079        
1080
1081        * Please refer to 
1082        https://pybaselines.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api/Baseline.html
1083        for more algorithms and their details.
1084
1085        Example
1086        -------
1087        
1088        >>> # Standard import
1089        >>> # (alternative: import ediff as ed => bkg is available as ed.bkg
1090        >>> import bground.api as bkg
1091        >>> 
1092        >>> # Define I/O files
1093        >>> IN  = r'orig_data.txt'
1094        >>> BKG = r'bkg-corrected_data.txt'
1095        >>> 
1096        >>> # Run BaseLines using a single command
1097        >>> BMET = bkg.Run.BaseLines(IN, BKG,
1098        >>>         xlabel='Pix', ylabel='Intensity',xlim=[0,200],ylim=180,
1099        >>>         algorithm="snip", xrange=(20, 200), decreasing=True)
1100        '''
1101
1102        DATA = InputData(in_data, sep=sep, usecols=usecols,
1103            comment=comment, skiprows=skiprows, header=header)
1104        PARS = BkgParams(bkg_file, 
1105             xlabel=xlabel, ylabel=ylabel, xlim=xlim, ylim=ylim,
1106             saveTXT=saveTXT, messages=messages)
1107
1108        BMET = BaseLines(DATA, PARS)
1109        BMET.run(algorithm, xrange, **kwargs)
1110
1111        return BMET
1112        
1113    
1114    def Wavelets():
1115        '''
1116        TODO: Adri ...
1117        '''
1118        pass
1119    
1120    
1121class Help():
1122    '''
1123    Class providing simple access to help functions.
1124    
1125    * The help functions just print basic information on stdout.
1126    * The help funcs are in bground.help module and can be called from here.
1127    
1128    >>> import bground.api as bkg
1129    >>> bkg.Help.intro()
1130    >>> bkg.Help.more_help()
1131    >>> bkg.Help.InteractivePlot()
1132    '''
1133    
1134    
1135    def intelligent_dedent(text: str) -> str:
1136        '''
1137        Intelligent dedentation - print multi-line string reasonably.
1138
1139        Parameters
1140        ----------
1141        text : str
1142            A multi-line string to print.
1143            The initial Python indentation is ignored (Python dedentation).
1144            The internal string indentation is preserved (internal indentation).
1145
1146        Returns
1147        -------
1148        str
1149            Processed string after inteligent dedentation.
1150            
1151        Technical notes
1152        ---------------
1153        * This function is used in printed help.
1154        * The printed help messages are typically multiline texts.
1155        '''
1156        
1157        # The following algorithm created with AI/ChGPT.
1158        # The code was slightly simplified - extra commands removed.
1159        # Original comments were improved + additional comments were added.
1160        
1161        # Split {text} into lines and ignore empty leading/trailing ones
1162        # (note #1: line.strip() returns falsi if it is an empty line
1163        # (note #2: the algorithm can delete 
1164        lines = text.splitlines()
1165        # Remove possible empty line(s) at the beginning
1166        while lines and not lines[0].strip():
1167            lines.pop(0)
1168        # Remove possible empty line(s) at the end
1169        while lines and not lines[-1].strip():
1170            lines.pop()
1171        # Return empty string if the multiline string contained just empty lines
1172        if not lines:
1173            return ""
1174
1175        # Find minimum indentation (tabs or spaces) among non-empty lines.
1176        # (note #1: line.strip() returns false if it is an empty line
1177        # (note #2: re.match(r'^[\t]*',line).groupt(0) = initial tabs = \t chars
1178        # (... line with two tabs contains \t\t at the beginning = 2 chars
1179        # (... the same is done for spaces => we use r'^[ \t]*' - space is there
1180        indent_levels = [
1181            len(re.match(r'^[ \t]*', line).group(0))
1182            for line in lines if line.strip()
1183        ]
1184        min_indent = min(indent_levels)
1185
1186        # Remove {min_indent} characters from the start of each line
1187        # (this is where some unnecessary/extra commands were removed
1188        dedented = [ line[min_indent:] for line in lines ]
1189        return("\n".join(dedented))
1190  
1191    
1192    def intro():
1193        '''
1194        BGROUND printed help :: Brief introduction
1195        '''
1196        
1197        help_text = '''
1198        =====================================================================
1199        BGROUND :: (semi)automated background subtraction for XY-data
1200        ---------------------------------------------------------------------
1201        * input data = XY-data (two columns)
1202            - text file or object with two (or more) columns
1203            - one of the columns = X-data, some other column = Y-data
1204            - allowed types of input (user specified input types and columns)
1205              text file, np.array or pd.DataFrame or ediff.io.Profile
1206        * output data = XY-data (four columns)
1207            - text file(s) and/or ediff.io.Profile object with four columns
1208            - cols in the text file: X, Y=Iraw, Ibkg, I=(Iraw-Ibkg)
1209            - cols in ELD = Profile: ELD.Pixels, ELD.Iraw, ELD.Ibkg, ELD.I 
1210        * semi-automated background subtraction:
1211            - computer opens an interactive plot
1212            - user defines background points with a mouse and keyboard
1213            - computer calculates the background + saves it as/when requested
1214        * fully-automated background subtraction:
1215            - user selects a method for background subtraction
1216            - computer calculates tha background and saves the output data
1217            - the output data are saved to file(s) and/or ediff.io.Profile
1218        * ediff.io.Profile
1219            - a specific type of input/output data
1220            - an object comming from our super-package ediff
1221            - the object is a 1D-profile from powder electron diffractogram 
1222        =====================================================================
1223        '''
1224        
1225        print(Help.intelligent_dedent(help_text))
1226        
1227    
1228    def more_help():
1229        '''
1230        BGROUND printed help :: Where to find additional help
1231        '''
1232        
1233        help_text = '''
1234        ==================================================================
1235        BGROUND package :: where to find more help
1236        ------------------------------------------------------------------
1237        Documentation + examples in www:
1238        * GitHub pages : https://mirekslouf.github.io/bground
1239        * GitHub docum : https://mirekslouf.github.io/bground/docs
1240        ------------------------------------------------------------------
1241        Additional help to the individual bkgr subtraction methods:
1242        >>> import bground.api as bkg
1243        >>> bkg.InteractivePlot.Help.how_it_works()
1244        ------------------------------------------------------------------
1245        Alternative access to help functions within ediff package
1246        >>> import ediff as ed
1247        >>> ed.bkg.InteractivePlot.Help.how_it_works()
1248        ==================================================================
1249        '''
1250        
1251        print(Help.intelligent_dedent(help_text))
1252
1253
1254    def InteractivePlot():
1255        '''
1256        BGROUND printed help :: InteractivePlot
1257        '''
1258        
1259        help_text = '''
1260        ===============================================================
1261        BGROUND :: InteractivePlot :: How it works
1262        ---------------------------------------------------------------
1263        * BGROUND opens Matplotlib interactive plot
1264        * the user defines backround points with mouse and keyboard
1265        * mouse actions/events are Matplotlib UI defaults
1266        * keyboard shortcuts/actions/events are defined by the program
1267          - keys for background definition: 1,2,3,4,5,6
1268          - keys for saving the results   : a,b,t,s,u
1269          - basic help is printed when the interactive plot opens
1270        --------------------------------------------------------------
1271        Complete help on keyboard shortcuts with detailed explanation:
1272        >>> import bground.api as bkg
1273        >>> bkg.InteractivePlot.Help.keyboard_shortcuts()
1274        --------------------------------------------------------------
1275        Alternative access to help from EDIFF package:
1276        >>> import ediff as ed
1277        >>> ed.bkg.InteractivePlot.Help.keyboard_shortcuts()
1278        ===============================================================
1279        '''
1280        
1281        print(Help.intelligent_dedent(help_text))
1282    
1283    
1284    def InteractivePlot_shortcuts( output_file='some_file' ):
1285        '''
1286        BGROUND printed help :: InteractivePlot :: Keyboard shortcuts
1287        '''
1288        
1289        # (1) Define output file names
1290        # (objective: all should have correct extensions
1291        # (but we want to avoid double TXT extension for the main TXT file
1292        TXTfile = output_file
1293        BKGfile = output_file + '.bp'
1294        PNGfile = output_file + '.png'
1295        if not(TXTfile.lower().endswith('.txt')): TXTfile = TXTfile + '.txt'
1296        
1297        # (2) Print help including the above defined output file names
1298        
1299        help_text = f'''
1300        ============================================================
1301        BGROUND :: Interactive plot :: Keyboard shortcuts
1302        ------------------------------------------------------------
1303        1 = add a background point (at the mouse cursor position)
1304        2 = delete a background point (close to the mouse cursor)
1305        3 = show the plot with all background points
1306        4 = show the plot with linear spline background
1307        5 = show the plot with quadratic spline background
1308        6 = show the plot with cubic spline background
1309        ------------------------------------------------------------
1310        a = background points :: load the previously saved
1311        b = background points :: save to BKG-file'
1312        (BKG-file = {BKGfile}
1313        --------
1314        s = save current plot to PNG-file:
1315        (PNG-file = {PNGfile}
1316        (note: Matplotlib UI shortcut; optional output
1317        --------
1318        t = subtract current background & save data to TXT-file
1319        (TXT-file = {TXTfile}
1320        (note: this is a universal output, applicable in all cases
1321        --------
1322        u = subtract current background & update ediff.io.Profile
1323        (ediff.io.Profile = (alternative) object with i/o data
1324        (note: the object is from ediff package; ignore if not used
1325        ------------------------------------------------------------
1326        Standard Matplotlib UI tools and shortcuts work as well.
1327        See: https://matplotlib.org/stable/users/interactive.html
1328        ============================================================
1329        '''
1330        
1331        print(Help.intelligent_dedent(help_text))
1332        
1333

1371        
1372class Plots:
1373    '''
1374    Class defining plotting for all background correction methods.
1375    
1376    * The class returns Plots object.
1377    * The {Plots} object is designed as a component
1378      for arbitrary background subtraction class/method.
1379    
1380    Example
1381    
1382    >>> import bground.api as bkg
1383    >>> IN_FILE  = r'input_data.txt'
1384    >>> BKG_FILE = r'processed_data.txt'
1385    >>> SMET = bkg.Run.InteractivePlot(IN_FILE, BKG_FILE)
1386    >>> SMET.plots.plot_original()
1387    >>> SMET.plots.plot_without_bkg() 
1388    
1389    How is it done?
1390    
1391    * Using true *composition* (not just aggregation).
1392    * The component class (Plots) holds the reference to the parent class.
1393    * The parent/composite class can be any background subtraction class,
1394      such as: InteractivePlot, RestoreFromPoints, ...
1395    * The parent class (for example InteractivePlot)
1396      saves the reference to Plots during initialization
1397      by means of the following command: `self.plots = Plots(self)`. 
1398    '''
1399    
1400        
1401    def __init__(self, parent):
1402        '''
1403        Initialize {Plots} object.
1404
1405        * The {Plots} object is designed as a component
1406          for arbitrary background subtraction class/method.
1407        * The method is typically not initialized directly,
1408          but within the composite/parent class that defines
1409          a bacgkround subtraction method.
1410    
1411        Parameters
1412        ----------
1413        parent : class defining a background subtraction method
1414            An arbitrary class in this module
1415            that defines a backround subtraction method, such as:
1416            InteractivePlot, RestoreFromPoints ...
1417
1418        Returns
1419        -------
1420        Plots object
1421            The object has the only property: `self._parent = parent`.
1422            This is the whole trick that makes this class a true component.
1423        '''
1424        self._parent = parent   # aggregated object
1425
1426    
1427    @staticmethod
1428    def set_plot_params(size=(10,5), dpi=100, fontsize=8, 
1429                        my_defaults=True, my_rcParams=None):
1430        '''
1431        Set global plot parameters.
1432        
1433        * This is a static method.
1434        * Mostly for plotting in Jupyter.
1435        * It may be used internally in bkg subtraction methods,
1436          but InteractivePlot uses its own settings of global plot parameters.
1437
1438        Parameters
1439        ----------
1440        size : tuple of two floats, optional, the default is (8,6)
1441            Size of the figure (width, height) in [cm].
1442        dpi : int, optional, the defalut is 100
1443            DPI of the figure.
1444        fontsize : int, optional, the default is 8
1445            Size of the font used in figure labels etc.
1446        my_defaults : bool, optional, default is True
1447            If True, some reasonable additional defaults are set,
1448            namely line widths and formats.
1449        my_rcParams : dict, optional, default is None
1450            Dictionary in plt.rcParams format
1451            containing any other allowed global plot parameters.
1452
1453        Returns
1454        -------
1455        None
1456            The result is a modification of the global plt.rcParams variable.
1457        '''
1458        # (1) Basic arguments -----------------------------------------------------
1459        if size:  # Figure size
1460            # Convert size in [cm] to required size in [inch]
1461            size = (size[0]/2.54, size[1]/2.54)
1462            plt.rcParams.update({'figure.figsize' : size})
1463        if dpi:  # Figure dpi
1464            plt.rcParams.update({'figure.dpi' : dpi})
1465        if fontsize:  # Global font size
1466            plt.rcParams.update({'font.size' : fontsize})
1467        # (2) Additional default parameters ---------------------------------------
1468        if my_defaults:  # Default rcParams if not forbidden by my_defaults=False
1469            plt.rcParams.update({
1470                'lines.linewidth'    : 0.8,
1471                'axes.linewidth'     : 0.6,
1472                'xtick.major.width'  : 0.6,
1473                'ytick.major.width'  : 0.6,
1474                'grid.linewidth'     : 0.6,
1475                'grid.linestyle'     : ':'})
1476        # (3) Further user-defined parameter in rcParams format -------------------
1477        if my_rcParams:  # Other possible rcParams in the form of dictionary
1478            plt.rcParams.update(my_rcParams)
1479
1480
1481    def plot_original(self, title='Raw data before processing', grid=True):
1482        '''
1483        Plot raw XY-data BEFORE any processing.
1484        
1485        Parameters
1486        ----------
1487        title : str, optional
1488            Title of the plot.
1489            The default can be changed to None (no title) or any other string.
1490        grid : bool, optional, default is True
1491            If true, show grid in the plot.
1492        
1493        Returns
1494        -------
1495        None
1496            The result is the plot shown on the screen.
1497        '''        
1498        # Close all previous plots.
1499        # (necessary to avoid confusions about current plot in Jupyter
1500        plt.close('all')
1501        # Get XY-data
1502        X,Y = self._parent.data[0:2]
1503        # PLot XY-data
1504        plt.plot(X,Y, 'b-')
1505        if title is not None: plt.title(title)
1506        # ...add xy-labels and limits
1507        plt.xlabel(self._parent.pars.xlabel)
1508        plt.ylabel(self._parent.pars.ylabel)
1509        plt.xlim(self._parent.pars.xlim)
1510        plt.ylim (self._parent.pars.ylim)
1511        # ...add grid
1512        if grid is not None: plt.grid()
1513        # Show the final plot
1514        plt.tight_layout()
1515        plt.show()
1516
1517    
1518    def plot_with_bkg(       
1519            self, title='Data with background definition', grid=True):
1520        '''
1521        Plot XY-data and background AFTER the interactive plot is closed.
1522
1523        Parameters
1524        ----------
1525        title : str, optional
1526            Title of the plot.
1527            The default can be changed to None (no title) or any other string.
1528        grid : bool, optional, default is True
1529            If true, show grid in the plot.
1530
1531        Returns
1532        -------
1533        None
1534            The result is the plot shown on the screen.
1535        '''
1536        
1537        # (0) Initialize, prepare parameters for plotting
1538        # Close all previous plots.
1539        # (necessary to avoid confusions about current plot in Jupyter
1540        plt.close('all')
1541        # Verify, if the background points are defined
1542        # (some methods use bkg points, some not
1543        # (below we use and plot the bkg points only if they are defined
1544        background_points_defined = len(self._parent.background.points.X) > 0
1545        # Get XY-data
1546        X,Y = self._parent.data[0:2]
1547        # Get background points => if they are defined!
1548        if background_points_defined:
1549            Xp = self._parent.background.points.X
1550            Yp = self._parent.background.points.Y
1551        # Get background interpolation curve
1552        Xc = self._parent.background.curve.X
1553        Yc = self._parent.background.curve.Y
1554        
1555        # (1) Plot data
1556        # ... XY-data
1557        plt.plot(X,Y, 'b-')
1558        # ... background interpolation curve
1559        plt.plot(Xc,Yc, 'r--')
1560        # ... background points => if they are defined!
1561        if background_points_defined: plt.plot(Xp,Yp, 'r+')
1562        
1563        # (2) Finalize the plot
1564        # ...add title
1565        if title is not None: plt.title(title)
1566        # ...add xy-labels and limits
1567        plt.xlabel(self._parent.pars.xlabel)
1568        plt.ylabel(self._parent.pars.ylabel)
1569        plt.xlim(self._parent.pars.xlim)
1570        plt.ylim (self._parent.pars.ylim)
1571        # ...add grid
1572        if grid is not None: plt.grid()
1573        
1574        # (3) Show the finalized plot
1575        plt.tight_layout()
1576        plt.show()
1577
1578    
1579    def plot_without_bkg(
1580            self, title='Data after background subtraction', 
1581            xlim=None, ylim=None, grid=True):
1582        '''
1583        Show background-corrected XY-data AFTER the interactive plot is closed.
1584
1585        Parameters
1586        ----------
1587        title : str, optional
1588            Title of the plot.
1589            The default can be changed to None (no title) or any other string.
1590        xlim : tuple or list with two values, default is None
1591            X-axis limits [xmin,xmax].
1592            If the default value is unchanged,
1593            the limits are taken from self.ppar.xlim.
1594        ylim : tuple or list with two values, default is None
1595            Y-axis limits [ymin,ymax].
1596            If the default value is unchanged,
1597            the limits are taken from self.ppar.xlim.
1598        grid : bool, optional, default is True
1599            If the argument is True, a grid is added to the plot.
1600
1601        Returns
1602        -------
1603        None
1604            The result is the plot shown on the screen.
1605        '''
1606        
1607        # Close all previous plots.
1608        # (necessary to avoid confusions about current plot in Jupyter
1609        plt.close('all')
1610        
1611        # Re-subtract background from points => if the points exist!
1612        # (just to be sure, it is quite fast
1613        # (only for methods that use background.points
1614        if len(self._parent.background.points.X) > 0:
1615            # If there are background points defined, recalculate.
1616            # (not necessary to save result => auto-saved in self._parent.data
1617            bground.points.bfunc.calculate_bkg_data(self._parent)
1618        
1619        # Define X,Y (X-coordinate, Intensity after bkg subtraction)
1620        # (the data have been recalculated above
1621        # (OR they should be present from automated bkg subtraction methods
1622        X,Y = self._parent.data[0], self._parent.data[3]
1623        
1624        # Plot background-corrected XY-data
1625        plt.plot(X,Y, 'b-')
1626        # ...add title
1627        if title is not None:
1628            plt.title(title)
1629        # ...add xy-labels
1630        plt.xlabel(self._parent.pars.xlabel)
1631        plt.ylabel(self._parent.pars.ylabel)
1632        # ...add xy-limits
1633        if xlim is None: xlim = self._parent.pars.xlim
1634        if ylim is None: ylim = self._parent.pars.ylim
1635        plt.xlim(xlim)
1636        plt.ylim(ylim)
1637        # ...add grid
1638        if grid is True: plt.grid()
1639        # Show the final plot
1640        plt.tight_layout()
1641        plt.show()
class InputData: 
 54class InputData:
 55    '''
 56    Class defining {InputData} for background subtraction.
 57    
 58    * Input data can be:
 59      filename, np.array, pd.DataFrame, or ediff.io.Diffractogram1D object.
 60    * In any case, the input data should contain two columns:
 61      `[X, Y = Iraw = raw_intensity]`.
 62    '''
 63    
 64    def __init__(self, input_data, **kwargs):
 65        '''
 66        Initialize {InputData} object that defines input for bkg subtraction.
 67
 68        Parameters
 69        ----------
 70        input_data : str or np.array or pd.DataFrame or ediff.io.Diffraction1D
 71            The input_data are converted to
 72            self.name, self.data and self.diff1D sub-objects.
 73            Self.name = name of the input file (or description of the source).
 74            Self.data = np.array with 2 rows: `[X, Y = Iraw = raw intensity]`.
 75            Self.diff1D = None or ediff.io.Diffraction1D object
 76            if it was given as an argument.
 77        kwargs : keyword arguments for pd.read_csv, optional
 78            The keyword arguments are relevant only if {input_data}
 79            is str/PathLike object defining the input data file.
 80            The keyword arguments are passed to pd.read_csv function
 81            to read the file as a simple DataFrame, which is immediately
 82            converted to a simple np.array = to self.data sub-object
 83            described above.
 84
 85        Returns
 86        -------
 87        bground.api.InputData
 88            The object is fully initialized
 89            and ready to use in background subtraction methods.
 90            It contains the self.name,
 91            self.data and (optionally) self.diff1D sub-objects described above.
 92            
 93        Technical notes
 94        ---------------
 95        * If {input_data} is a file, we read it to np.array with
 96          pd.read_csv instead of np.loadtxt, as it is more flexible/powerful.
 97        * The {kwargs} are pre-set reasonably enough to read most of common
 98          files, but user can adjuste them if needed - of course.
 99        * If {kwargs} need to be adjusted, see the comments in the source
100          code and pandas.read_csv online documentation.
101        '''
102        
103        # Initialization of InputData object.
104        # * Two pieces of input:
105        #   input_data (file or array or DataFrame or ediff.io.Diffraction1D)
106        #   kwargs (to read the file using pd.read_csv function
107        # * The input is saved in two/three properties:
108        #   self.name = name of the input file (or str describing data source)
109        #   self.data = the XY-data itself = array with 2 rows [X,Y=Intensity]
110        #   self.diff1D = extra object if ediff.io.Diffracton1D was the input
111        #                 this is set to None if the input was "common data"
112        self.name, self.data, self.diff1D = \
113            InputData.read_input_data(input_data, **kwargs)
114        
115    
116    @staticmethod
117    def read_input_data(input_data, **kwargs):
118        '''
119        Read {input_data} using optional {kwargs}
120        return the results to be saved in {InputData} object properties.
121    
122        * intrinsic method of {InputData} object
123        * more info => look at the detailed comments in the func source code
124        '''
125        # Read input data with XY-data.
126        # No docstring: just class description above + comments below.
127        # * The optional arguments (comment, sep, usecols)
128        #   are passed to pandas.read_csv if the input_data ~ filename.
129    
130        if isinstance(input_data, (str, Path)):
131            # The input is a file = filename given be str or PathLike var.
132            # => read the file using pd.read_csv
133            # => user's responsibility: modify optional args as needed
134            #    the optional arguments are: comment, sep, usecols
135            #    these arguments are passed to pd.read_csv
136            # (a) set name = filename
137            name = str(input_data)
138            # (b) get **kwargs for pd.read_csv OR set reasonable defaults
139            # ... comments may be present
140            my_comment  = kwargs.get('comment') or '#'
141            # ... header should be None if there are no column headers!
142            my_header   = kwargs.get('header') or 'infer'
143            # ... skiprows can be defined for completness and flexibility
144            my_skiprows = kwargs.get('skiprows') or 0
145            # ... separator can be defined, defalt is any whitespace
146            my_sep      = kwargs.get('sep') or r'\s+'
147            # ... usecols may be defined, default is the first two columns
148            my_usecols  = kwargs.get('usecols') or [0,1]
149            # ... columns may be re-defined OR default column names are used
150            my_columns  = kwargs.get('columns') or None
151            # (c) read the file with pd.read_csv and the **kwargs from above 
152            df = pd.read_csv(input_data, 
153                comment=my_comment, header=my_header, skiprows=my_skiprows, 
154                sep=my_sep, usecols=my_usecols)
155            if my_columns is not None: df.columns = my_columns
156            # (d) convert pd.DataFrame to np.ndarray
157            #     and transpose the array (columns should be the 1st index)
158            data = np.transpose(np.array(df))
159            # (e) set that the data are NOT the special ed.io.Profile
160            diff1D = None
161        elif isinstance(input_data, np.ndarray):
162            # The input is a numpy array.
163            # => just assign array to data
164            # => user's responsibility: ensure that the numpy.array
165            #    contains just two rows: X-values and Y-values
166            #    (for example, pass something like: arr[[0,2]]
167            name = 'np.ndarray'
168            data = input_data
169            diff1D = None
170        elif type(input_data) == pd.DataFrame:
171            # The input is pandas.DataFrame
172            # => convert DataFrame to numpy.array
