1002        '''
1003        Run bground.api.RestoreFromPoints method with a single func/command.
1004        
1005        TODO: docs => Adri
1006        '''
1007        
1008        # (1) Define objects with input and output data
1009        DATA = InputData(in_data, sep=sep, usecols=usecols,
1010            comment=comment, skiprows=skiprows, header=header)
1011        PARS = BkgParams(bkg_file, 
1012             xlabel=xlabel, ylabel=ylabel, xlim=xlim, ylim=ylim,
1013             saveTXT=saveTXT, messages=messages)
1014        
1015        # (2) Define the method
1016        # (including optional {out_file} to specify
1017        SMET = SimpleFuncs(DATA, PARS)
1018        
1019        # (3) Run the method
1020        SMET.run(algorithm, **kwargs)
1021        
1022        # (4) Return the final RestoreFromPoints object
1023        # (the data are auto-saved to bkg_file(s)
1024        # (BUT returning the object is useful to see/show/plot the data
1025        return(SMET)
1026
1027
1028    def BaseLines(in_data, bkg_file=None, saveTXT=True,
1029            comment='#', skiprows=0, header='infer', sep=r'\s+', usecols=[0,1], 
1030            xlabel=None, ylabel=None, xlim=None, ylim=None, messages=False,
1031            algorithm="peak_filling", xrange=None, **kwargs):
1032        '''
1033        Run bground.api.BaseLines method with a single function/command.
1034
1035        Parameters
1036        ----------
1037        in_data : filename or np.ndarray or pd.DataFrame or ELD profile
1038            Input XYdata, containing 2 columns:
1039            `[X, Y = Iraw = raw intensity]`.
1040        bkg_file : filename
1041            Name of the file with background points.
1042            According to bground package convention,
1043            the filename should be `something.txt` or `something.txt.bp`.
1044            TXT-files contain the XY-data
1045            and TXT.BP-files contain background points.
1046            If {bkg_file} is a TXT file, the extension is converted to TXT.BP.
1047        saveTXT : bool, optional, default is True
1048            If True (default), save results in both TXT-file and self.diff1D.
1049            It can be switched to False in certain automated processing paths.            
1050        comment, skiprows, header, sep, usecols: params for pd.read_csv func
1051            Parameters that are passed to pd.read_csv function
1052            if the {in_data} is an XYfile with two columns.
1053            See bground.api.InputData docs for more details.
1054         xlabel, ylabel, xlim, ylim, messages, CLI: params for plotting
1055            Parameters that are used when plotting the XYdata
1056            in the form of matplotlib interactive graph.
1057            See bground.api.BkgParams docs for more details.
1058        algorithm : str, optional, by default "peak_filling"
1059            Algorithm from pybaselines for baseline detection.        
1060        xrange : tuple with two floats, optional, by default None
1061            A range on the x axis, where the baseline should be subtracted.
1062            The range is inclusive. If None, the whole range is used.
1063
1064        Returns
1065        -------
1066        bground.api.BaseLines
1067            The object is used to run the BaseLines method,
1068            but it also saves and plots the original and bkg-corrected data.
1069
1070        Notes
1071        -----
1072        * `kwargs` are passed to the algorithm.
1073
1074        * Recommended algorithms (and parameters in kwargs):
1075            * `peak_filling` (with parameter `half_window=1`),
1076            * `snip` (with parameter `decreasing=True`),
1077            * `pspline_iasls` (with parameter `lam=5`), 
1078            * `irsqr` (with parameter `lam=1000`),
1079            * `rubberband` (with parameter `lam=2`)
1080        
1081
1082        * Please refer to 
1083        https://pybaselines.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api/Baseline.html
1084        for more algorithms and their details.
1085
1086        Example
1087        -------
1088        
1089        >>> # Standard import
1090        >>> # (alternative: import ediff as ed => bkg is available as ed.bkg
1091        >>> import bground.api as bkg
1092        >>> 
1093        >>> # Define I/O files
1094        >>> IN  = r'orig_data.txt'
1095        >>> BKG = r'bkg-corrected_data.txt'
1096        >>> 
1097        >>> # Run BaseLines using a single command
1098        >>> BMET = bkg.Run.BaseLines(IN, BKG,
1099        >>>         xlabel='Pix', ylabel='Intensity',xlim=[0,200],ylim=180,
1100        >>>         algorithm="snip", xrange=(20, 200), decreasing=True)
1101        '''
1102
1103        DATA = InputData(in_data, sep=sep, usecols=usecols,
1104            comment=comment, skiprows=skiprows, header=header)
1105        PARS = BkgParams(bkg_file, 
1106             xlabel=xlabel, ylabel=ylabel, xlim=xlim, ylim=ylim,
1107             saveTXT=saveTXT, messages=messages)
1108
1109        BMET = BaseLines(DATA, PARS)
1110        BMET.run(algorithm, xrange, **kwargs)
1111
1112        return BMET
1113        
1114    
1115    def Wavelets():
1116        '''
1117        TODO: Adri ...
1118        '''
1119        pass

The class with funcs to run each bkg subtraction method in one step.

  • Funcs in this class initialize and run a selected method in one step.
  • They set bground.api.InputData and bground.api.BkgParams and run the method.
  • All parameters are set in one place, which is convenient for the modern/simple OO-processing.

List of the available methods:

  • Run.InteractivePlot = run {InteractivePlot} bkg subtraction method
  • Run.RestoreFromPoints = run {RestoreFromPoints} bkg subtration method
  • Run.SimpleFuncs = run {SimpleFuncs} bkg subtraction method(s)
  • Run.BaseLines = run {BaseLines} bkg subtraction method(s)
  • Run.Wavelets = run {Wavelets} bkg subtraction method(s)
def InteractivePlot( in_data, bkg_file=None, comment='#', skiprows=0, header='infer', sep='\\s+', usecols=[0, 1], xlabel=None, ylabel=None, xlim=None, ylim=None, saveTXT=True, messages=True, CLI=False): 
838    def InteractivePlot(
839            in_data, bkg_file=None,
840            comment='#', skiprows=0, header='infer', sep=r'\s+', usecols=[0,1], 
841            xlabel=None, ylabel=None, xlim=None, ylim=None, 
842            saveTXT=True, messages=True, CLI=False):
843        '''
844        Run bground.api.InteractivePlot method with a single function/command.
845               
846        Parameters
847        ----------
848        in_data : filename or np.ndarray or pd.DataFrame or ELD profile
849            Input XYdata, containing 2 columns:
850            `[X, Y = Iraw = raw intensity]`.
851        bkg_file : filename, optional, default is None
852            Name of the background/output file(s).
853            There can be three different output files:
854            data file ({bkg_file}.txt),
855            background points ({bkg_file}.txt.bp),
856            background plot ({bkg_file}.txt.png).
857            If {bkg_file} is not specified (None) and {saveTXT}=False,
858            then the TXT-files are not created and the results are
859            saved only within the InteractivePlot object
860            (in self.data, self.background, and possibly self.diff1D).
861            The self.diff1D sub-object is saved only if the input is
862            a ediff.io.Diffractogram1D object.
863        comment, skiprows, header, sep, usecols: params for pd.read_csv func
864             Parameters that are passed to pd.read_csv function
865             if the {in_data} is an XYfile with two columns.
866             See bground.api.InputData docs for more details.
867         xlabel, ylabel, xlim, ylim: params for plotting
868             Parameters that are used when plotting the XYdata
869             in the form of matplotlib interactive graph.
870             See bground.api.BkgParams docs for more details.
871        saveTXT : bool, optional, default is True
872            If True (default), save results in both TXT-file and self.diff1D.
873            It can be switched to False in certain automated processing paths.            
874        messages : bool, optional, default is True
875            If True (default), print short messages on stdout.
876        CLI : bool, optional, default is False
877            Must be set to True when program runs from pure Python in CLI.
878            It ensures that the interactive plot will not close immediately.
879        
880        Returns
881        -------
882        bground.api.InteractivePlot
883            The object is used to run the InteractivePlot method,
884            but it also saves and plots the original and bkg-corrected data.
885        
886        Example
887        -------
888        
889        >>> # Standard import
890        >>> # (alternative: import ediff as ed => bkg is available as ed.bkg
891        >>> import bground.api as bkg
892        >>>
893        >>> # Define I/O files
894        >>> # (input file  = XYdata, 2 cols: [X, Y = Iraw = Raw Intensity]
895        >>> # (output file = XYdata, 4 cols: [X, Iraw, Inet, I = (Iraw - Inet)]
896        >>> IN  = 'data1_raw_intensity.txt'
897        >>> OUT = 'data2_bkg_subtracted.txt'
898        >>>
899        >>> # Run InteractivePlot subtraction method with a single function
900        >>> # (the func initializes all objects and runs the method
901        >>> # (SMET.data + SMET.background sub-objects will contain the results
902        >>> SMET = bkg.Run.InteractivePlot(IN, OUT, 
903        >>>     xlabel='Pixels', ylabel='Intensity', xlim=300, ylim=300)
904        '''
905        
906        # (1) Define objects with input and output data
907        DATA = InputData(in_data, sep=sep, usecols=usecols,
908            comment=comment, skiprows=skiprows, header=header)
909        PARS = BkgParams(bkg_file, 
910            xlabel=xlabel, ylabel=ylabel, xlim=xlim, ylim=ylim,
911            messages=messages, saveTXT=saveTXT)
912        
913        # (2) Define the method
914        # (including optional argument CLI if it runs from pure CLI python
915        SMET = InteractivePlot(DATA, PARS, CLI=CLI)
916        
917        # (3) Run the method
918        SMET.run()
919        
920        # (4) Return the final InteractivePlot object
921        # (the data are auto-saved to bkg_file(s)
922        # (BUT returning the object is useful to see/show/plot the data
923        return(SMET)

Run bground.api.InteractivePlot method with a single function/command.

Parameters
  • in_data (filename or np.ndarray or pd.DataFrame or ELD profile): Input XYdata, containing 2 columns: [X, Y = Iraw = raw intensity].
  • bkg_file (filename, optional, default is None): Name of the background/output file(s). There can be three different output files: data file ({bkg_file}.txt), background points ({bkg_file}.txt.bp), background plot ({bkg_file}.txt.png). If {bkg_file} is not specified (None) and {saveTXT}=False, then the TXT-files are not created and the results are saved only within the InteractivePlot object (in self.data, self.background, and possibly self.diff1D). The self.diff1D sub-object is saved only if the input is a ediff.io.Diffractogram1D object.
  • comment, skiprows, header, sep, usecols (params for pd.read_csv func): Parameters that are passed to pd.read_csv function if the {in_data} is an XYfile with two columns. See bground.api.InputData docs for more details. xlabel, ylabel, xlim, ylim: params for plotting Parameters that are used when plotting the XYdata in the form of matplotlib interactive graph. See bground.api.BkgParams docs for more details.
  • saveTXT (bool, optional, default is True): If True (default), save results in both TXT-file and self.diff1D. It can be switched to False in certain automated processing paths.
  • messages (bool, optional, default is True): If True (default), print short messages on stdout.
  • CLI (bool, optional, default is False): Must be set to True when program runs from pure Python in CLI. It ensures that the interactive plot will not close immediately.
Returns
  • bground.api.InteractivePlot: The object is used to run the InteractivePlot method, but it also saves and plots the original and bkg-corrected data.
Example
>>> # Standard import
>>> # (alternative: import ediff as ed => bkg is available as ed.bkg
>>> import bground.api as bkg
>>>
>>> # Define I/O files
>>> # (input file  = XYdata, 2 cols: [X, Y = Iraw = Raw Intensity]
>>> # (output file = XYdata, 4 cols: [X, Iraw, Inet, I = (Iraw - Inet)]
>>> IN  = 'data1_raw_intensity.txt'
>>> OUT = 'data2_bkg_subtracted.txt'
>>>
>>> # Run InteractivePlot subtraction method with a single function
>>> # (the func initializes all objects and runs the method
>>> # (SMET.data + SMET.background sub-objects will contain the results
>>> SMET = bkg.Run.InteractivePlot(IN, OUT, 
>>>     xlabel='Pixels', ylabel='Intensity', xlim=300, ylim=300)
def RestoreFromPoints( in_data, bkg_file, saveTXT=True, comment='#', skiprows=0, header='infer', sep='\\s+', usecols=[0, 1], xlabel=None, ylabel=None, xlim=None, ylim=None, messages=False): 
926    def RestoreFromPoints(in_data, bkg_file, saveTXT=True,
927            comment='#', skiprows=0, header='infer', sep=r'\s+', usecols=[0,1], 
928            xlabel=None, ylabel=None, xlim=None, ylim=None, messages=False):
929        '''
930        Run bground.api.RestoreFromPoints method with a single func/command.
931
932        Parameters
933        ----------
934        in_data : filename or np.ndarray or pd.DataFrame or ELD profile
935            Input XYdata, containing 2 columns:
936            `[X, Y = Iraw = raw intensity]`.
937        bkg_file : filename
938            Name of the file with background points.
939            According to bground package convention,
940            the filename should be `something.txt` or `something.txt.bp`.
941            TXT-files contain the XY-data
942            and TXT.BP-files contain background points.
943            If {bkg_file} is a TXT file, the extension is converted to TXT.BP.
944        saveTXT : bool, optional, default is True
945            If True (default), save results in both TXT-file and self.diff1D.
946            It can be switched to False in certain automated processing paths.            
947        comment, skiprows, header, sep, usecols: params for pd.read_csv func
948            Parameters that are passed to pd.read_csv function
949            if the {in_data} is an XYfile with two columns.
950            See bground.api.InputData docs for more details.
951         xlabel, ylabel, xlim, ylim, messages, CLI: params for plotting
952            Parameters that are used when plotting the XYdata
953            in the form of matplotlib interactive graph.
954            See bground.api.BkgParams docs for more details.
955        
956        Returns
957        -------
958        bground.api.RestoreFromPoints
959            The object is used to run the RestoreFromPoints method,
960            but it also saves and plots the original and bkg-corrected data.
961        
962        Example
963        -------
964        
965        >>> # Standard import
966        >>> # (alternative: import ediff as ed => bkg is available as ed.bkg
967        >>> import bground.api as bkg
968        >>> 
969        >>> # Define I/O files
970        >>> IN  = r'orig_data.txt'
971        >>> BKG = r'bkg-corrected_data.txt'
972        >>> 
973        >>> # Run RestoreFromPoints using a single command
974        >>> SMET = bkg.Run.RestoreFromPoints(IN, BKG,
975        >>>         xlabel='Pix', ylabel='Intensity',xlim=[0,200],ylim=180)
976        '''
977        
978        # (1) Define objects with input and output data
979        DATA = InputData(in_data, sep=sep, usecols=usecols,
980            comment=comment, skiprows=skiprows, header=header)
981        PARS = BkgParams(bkg_file, 
982             xlabel=xlabel, ylabel=ylabel, xlim=xlim, ylim=ylim,
983             saveTXT=saveTXT, messages=messages)
984        
985        # (2) Define the method
986        # (including optional {out_file} to specify 
987        SMET = RestoreFromPoints(DATA, PARS, CLI=False)
988        
989        # (3) Run the method
990        SMET.run()
991        
992        # (4) Return the final RestoreFromPoints object
993        # (the data are auto-saved to bkg_file(s)
994        # (BUT returning the object is useful to see/show/plot the data
995        return(SMET)

Run bground.api.RestoreFromPoints method with a single func/command.

Parameters
  • in_data (filename or np.ndarray or pd.DataFrame or ELD profile): Input XYdata, containing 2 columns: [X, Y = Iraw = raw intensity].
  • bkg_file (filename): Name of the file with background points. According to bground package convention, the filename should be something.txt or something.txt.bp. TXT-files contain the XY-data and TXT.BP-files contain background points. If {bkg_file} is a TXT file, the extension is converted to TXT.BP.
  • saveTXT (bool, optional, default is True): If True (default), save results in both TXT-file and self.diff1D. It can be switched to False in certain automated processing paths.
  • comment, skiprows, header, sep, usecols (params for pd.read_csv func): Parameters that are passed to pd.read_csv function if the {in_data} is an XYfile with two columns. See bground.api.InputData docs for more details. xlabel, ylabel, xlim, ylim, messages, CLI: params for plotting Parameters that are used when plotting the XYdata in the form of matplotlib interactive graph. See bground.api.BkgParams docs for more details.
Returns
  • bground.api.RestoreFromPoints: The object is used to run the RestoreFromPoints method, but it also saves and plots the original and bkg-corrected data.
Example
>>> # Standard import
>>> # (alternative: import ediff as ed => bkg is available as ed.bkg
>>> import bground.api as bkg
>>> 
>>> # Define I/O files
>>> IN  = r'orig_data.txt'
>>> BKG = r'bkg-corrected_data.txt'
>>> 
>>> # Run RestoreFromPoints using a single command
>>> SMET = bkg.Run.RestoreFromPoints(IN, BKG,
>>>         xlabel='Pix', ylabel='Intensity',xlim=[0,200],ylim=180)
def SimpleFuncs( in_data, bkg_file=None, saveTXT=True, comment='#', skiprows=0, header='infer', sep='\\s+', usecols=[0, 1], xlabel=None, ylabel=None, xlim=None, ylim=None, messages=False, algorithm='RollingBall', **kwargs): 
 998    def SimpleFuncs(in_data, bkg_file=None, saveTXT=True,
 999            comment='#', skiprows=0, header='infer', sep=r'\s+', usecols=[0,1], 
1000            xlabel=None, ylabel=None, xlim=None, ylim=None, messages=False,
1001            algorithm='RollingBall', **kwargs):
1002        '''
1003        Run bground.api.RestoreFromPoints method with a single func/command.
1004        
1005        TODO: docs => Adri
1006        '''
1007        
1008        # (1) Define objects with input and output data
1009        DATA = InputData(in_data, sep=sep, usecols=usecols,
1010            comment=comment, skiprows=skiprows, header=header)
1011        PARS = BkgParams(bkg_file, 
1012             xlabel=xlabel, ylabel=ylabel, xlim=xlim, ylim=ylim,
1013             saveTXT=saveTXT, messages=messages)
1014        
1015        # (2) Define the method
1016        # (including optional {out_file} to specify
1017        SMET = SimpleFuncs(DATA, PARS)
1018        
1019        # (3) Run the method
1020        SMET.run(algorithm, **kwargs)
1021        
1022        # (4) Return the final RestoreFromPoints object
1023        # (the data are auto-saved to bkg_file(s)
1024        # (BUT returning the object is useful to see/show/plot the data
1025        return(SMET)

Run bground.api.RestoreFromPoints method with a single func/command.

TODO: docs => Adri

def BaseLines( in_data, bkg_file=None, saveTXT=True, comment='#', skiprows=0, header='infer', sep='\\s+', usecols=[0, 1], xlabel=None, ylabel=None, xlim=None, ylim=None, messages=False, algorithm='peak_filling', xrange=None, **kwargs): 
1028    def BaseLines(in_data, bkg_file=None, saveTXT=True,
1029            comment='#', skiprows=0, header='infer', sep=r'\s+', usecols=[0,1], 
1030            xlabel=None, ylabel=None, xlim=None, ylim=None, messages=False,
1031            algorithm="peak_filling", xrange=None, **kwargs):
1032        '''
1033        Run bground.api.BaseLines method with a single function/command.
1034
1035        Parameters
1036        ----------
1037        in_data : filename or np.ndarray or pd.DataFrame or ELD profile
1038            Input XYdata, containing 2 columns:
1039            `[X, Y = Iraw = raw intensity]`.
1040        bkg_file : filename
1041            Name of the file with background points.
1042            According to bground package convention,
1043            the filename should be `something.txt` or `something.txt.bp`.
1044            TXT-files contain the XY-data
1045            and TXT.BP-files contain background points.
1046            If {bkg_file} is a TXT file, the extension is converted to TXT.BP.
1047        saveTXT : bool, optional, default is True
1048            If True (default), save results in both TXT-file and self.diff1D.
1049            It can be switched to False in certain automated processing paths.            
1050        comment, skiprows, header, sep, usecols: params for pd.read_csv func
1051            Parameters that are passed to pd.read_csv function
1052            if the {in_data} is an XYfile with two columns.
1053            See bground.api.InputData docs for more details.
1054         xlabel, ylabel, xlim, ylim, messages, CLI: params for plotting
1055            Parameters that are used when plotting the XYdata
1056            in the form of matplotlib interactive graph.
1057            See bground.api.BkgParams docs for more details.
1058        algorithm : str, optional, by default "peak_filling"
1059            Algorithm from pybaselines for baseline detection.        
1060        xrange : tuple with two floats, optional, by default None
1061            A range on the x axis, where the baseline should be subtracted.
1062            The range is inclusive. If None, the whole range is used.
1063
1064        Returns
1065        -------
1066        bground.api.BaseLines
1067            The object is used to run the BaseLines method,
1068            but it also saves and plots the original and bkg-corrected data.
1069
1070        Notes
1071        -----
1072        * `kwargs` are passed to the algorithm.
1073
1074        * Recommended algorithms (and parameters in kwargs):
1075            * `peak_filling` (with parameter `half_window=1`),
1076            * `snip` (with parameter `decreasing=True`),
1077            * `pspline_iasls` (with parameter `lam=5`), 
1078            * `irsqr` (with parameter `lam=1000`),
1079            * `rubberband` (with parameter `lam=2`)
1080        
1081
1082        * Please refer to 
1083        https://pybaselines.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api/Baseline.html
1084        for more algorithms and their details.
1085
1086        Example
1087        -------
1088        
1089        >>> # Standard import
1090        >>> # (alternative: import ediff as ed => bkg is available as ed.bkg
1091        >>> import bground.api as bkg
1092        >>> 
1093        >>> # Define I/O files
1094        >>> IN  = r'orig_data.txt'
1095        >>> BKG = r'bkg-corrected_data.txt'
1096        >>> 
1097        >>> # Run BaseLines using a single command
1098        >>> BMET = bkg.Run.BaseLines(IN, BKG,
1099        >>>         xlabel='Pix', ylabel='Intensity',xlim=[0,200],ylim=180,
1100        >>>         algorithm="snip", xrange=(20, 200), decreasing=True)
1101        '''
1102
1103        DATA = InputData(in_data, sep=sep, usecols=usecols,
1104            comment=comment, skiprows=skiprows, header=header)
1105        PARS = BkgParams(bkg_file, 
1106             xlabel=xlabel, ylabel=ylabel, xlim=xlim, ylim=ylim,
1107             saveTXT=saveTXT, messages=messages)
1108
1109        BMET = BaseLines(DATA, PARS)
1110        BMET.run(algorithm, xrange, **kwargs)
1111
1112        return BMET

Run bground.api.BaseLines method with a single function/command.

Parameters
  • in_data (filename or np.ndarray or pd.DataFrame or ELD profile): Input XYdata, containing 2 columns: [X, Y = Iraw = raw intensity].
  • bkg_file (filename): Name of the file with background points. According to bground package convention, the filename should be something.txt or something.txt.bp. TXT-files contain the XY-data and TXT.BP-files contain background points. If {bkg_file} is a TXT file, the extension is converted to TXT.BP.
  • saveTXT (bool, optional, default is True): If True (default), save results in both TXT-file and self.diff1D. It can be switched to False in certain automated processing paths.
  • comment, skiprows, header, sep, usecols (params for pd.read_csv func): Parameters that are passed to pd.read_csv function if the {in_data} is an XYfile with two columns. See bground.api.InputData docs for more details. xlabel, ylabel, xlim, ylim, messages, CLI: params for plotting Parameters that are used when plotting the XYdata in the form of matplotlib interactive graph. See bground.api.BkgParams docs for more details.
  • algorithm (str, optional, by default "peak_filling"): Algorithm from pybaselines for baseline detection.
  • xrange (tuple with two floats, optional, by default None): A range on the x axis, where the baseline should be subtracted. The range is inclusive. If None, the whole range is used.
Returns
  • bground.api.BaseLines: The object is used to run the BaseLines method, but it also saves and plots the original and bkg-corrected data.
Notes

  • kwargs are passed to the algorithm.

  • Recommended algorithms (and parameters in kwargs):

    • peak_filling (with parameter half_window=1),
    • snip (with parameter decreasing=True),
    • pspline_iasls (with parameter lam=5),
    • irsqr (with parameter lam=1000),
    • rubberband (with parameter lam=2)

  • Please refer to https://pybaselines.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api/Baseline.html for more algorithms and their details.

Example
>>> # Standard import
>>> # (alternative: import ediff as ed => bkg is available as ed.bkg
>>> import bground.api as bkg
>>> 
>>> # Define I/O files
>>> IN  = r'orig_data.txt'
>>> BKG = r'bkg-corrected_data.txt'
>>> 
>>> # Run BaseLines using a single command
>>> BMET = bkg.Run.BaseLines(IN, BKG,
>>>         xlabel='Pix', ylabel='Intensity',xlim=[0,200],ylim=180,
>>>         algorithm="snip", xrange=(20, 200), decreasing=True)
def Wavelets(): 
1115    def Wavelets():
1116        '''
1117        TODO: Adri ...
1118        '''
1119        pass

TODO: Adri ...

class Help: 
1122class Help():
1123    '''
1124    Class providing simple access to help functions.
1125    
1126    * The help functions just print basic information on stdout.
1127    * The help funcs are in bground.help module and can be called from here.
1128    
1129    >>> import bground.api as bkg
1130    >>> bkg.Help.intro()
1131    >>> bkg.Help.more_help()
1132    >>> bkg.Help.InteractivePlot()
1133    '''
1134    
1135    
1136    def intelligent_dedent(text: str) -> str:
1137        '''
1138        Intelligent dedentation - print multi-line string reasonably.
1139
1140        Parameters
1141        ----------
1142        text : str
1143            A multi-line string to print.
1144            The initial Python indentation is ignored (Python dedentation).
1145            The internal string indentation is preserved (internal indentation).
1146
1147        Returns
1148        -------
1149        str
1150            Processed string after inteligent dedentation.
1151            
1152        Technical notes
1153        ---------------
1154        * This function is used in printed help.
1155        * The printed help messages are typically multiline texts.
1156        '''
1157        
1158        # The following algorithm created with AI/ChGPT.
1159        # The code was slightly simplified - extra commands removed.
1160        # Original comments were improved + additional comments were added.
1161        
1162        # Split {text} into lines and ignore empty leading/trailing ones
1163        # (note #1: line.strip() returns falsi if it is an empty line
1164        # (note #2: the algorithm can delete 
1165        lines = text.splitlines()
1166        # Remove possible empty line(s) at the beginning
1167        while lines and not lines[0].strip():
1168            lines.pop(0)
1169        # Remove possible empty line(s) at the end
1170        while lines and not lines[-1].strip():
1171            lines.pop()
1172        # Return empty string if the multiline string contained just empty lines
1173        if not lines:
1174            return ""
1175
1176        # Find minimum indentation (tabs or spaces) among non-empty lines.
1177        # (note #1: line.strip() returns false if it is an empty line
1178        # (note #2: re.match(r'^[\t]*',line).groupt(0) = initial tabs = \t chars
1179        # (... line with two tabs contains \t\t at the beginning = 2 chars
1180        # (... the same is done for spaces => we use r'^[ \t]*' - space is there
1181        indent_levels = [
1182            len(re.match(r'^[ \t]*', line).group(0))
1183            for line in lines if line.strip()
1184        ]
1185        min_indent = min(indent_levels)
1186
1187        # Remove {min_indent} characters from the start of each line
1188        # (this is where some unnecessary/extra commands were removed
1189        dedented = [ line[min_indent:] for line in lines ]
1190        return("\n".join(dedented))
1191  
1192    
1193    def intro():
1194        '''
1195        BGROUND printed help :: Brief introduction
1196        '''
1197        
1198        help_text = '''
1199        =====================================================================
1200        BGROUND :: (semi)automated background subtraction for XY-data
1201        ---------------------------------------------------------------------
1202        * input data = XY-data (two columns)
1203            - text file or object with two (or more) columns
1204            - one of the columns = X-data, some other column = Y-data
1205            - allowed types of input (user specified input types and columns)
1206              text file, np.array or pd.DataFrame or ediff.io.Profile
1207        * output data = XY-data (four columns)
1208            - text file(s) and/or ediff.io.Profile object with four columns
1209            - cols in the text file: X, Y=Iraw, Ibkg, I=(Iraw-Ibkg)
1210            - cols in ELD = Profile: ELD.Pixels, ELD.Iraw, ELD.Ibkg, ELD.I 
1211        * semi-automated background subtraction:
1212            - computer opens an interactive plot
1213            - user defines background points with a mouse and keyboard
1214            - computer calculates the background + saves it as/when requested
1215        * fully-automated background subtraction:
1216            - user selects a method for background subtraction
1217            - computer calculates tha background and saves the output data
1218            - the output data are saved to file(s) and/or ediff.io.Profile
1219        * ediff.io.Profile
1220            - a specific type of input/output data
1221            - an object comming from our super-package ediff
1222            - the object is a 1D-profile from powder electron diffractogram 
1223        =====================================================================
1224        '''
1225        
1226        print(Help.intelligent_dedent(help_text))
1227        
1228    
1229    def more_help():
1230        '''
1231        BGROUND printed help :: Where to find additional help
1232        '''
1233        
1234        help_text = '''
1235        ==================================================================
1236        BGROUND package :: where to find more help
1237        ------------------------------------------------------------------
1238        Documentation + examples in www:
1239        * GitHub pages : https://mirekslouf.github.io/bground
1240        * GitHub docum : https://mirekslouf.github.io/bground/docs
1241        ------------------------------------------------------------------
1242        Additional help to the individual bkgr subtraction methods:
1243        >>> import bground.api as bkg
1244        >>> bkg.InteractivePlot.Help.how_it_works()
1245        ------------------------------------------------------------------
1246        Alternative access to help functions within ediff package
1247        >>> import ediff as ed
1248        >>> ed.bkg.InteractivePlot.Help.how_it_works()
1249        ==================================================================
1250        '''
1251        
1252        print(Help.intelligent_dedent(help_text))
1253
1254
1255    def InteractivePlot():
1256        '''
1257        BGROUND printed help :: InteractivePlot
1258        '''
1259        
1260        help_text = '''
1261        ===============================================================
1262        BGROUND :: InteractivePlot :: How it works
1263        ---------------------------------------------------------------
1264        * BGROUND opens Matplotlib interactive plot
1265        * the user defines backround points with mouse and keyboard
1266        * mouse actions/events are Matplotlib UI defaults
1267        * keyboard shortcuts/actions/events are defined by the program
1268          - keys for background definition: 1,2,3,4,5,6
1269          - keys for saving the results   : a,b,t,s,u
1270          - basic help is printed when the interactive plot opens
1271        --------------------------------------------------------------
1272        Complete help on keyboard shortcuts with detailed explanation:
1273        >>> import bground.api as bkg
1274        >>> bkg.InteractivePlot.Help.keyboard_shortcuts()
1275        --------------------------------------------------------------
1276        Alternative access to help from EDIFF package:
1277        >>> import ediff as ed
1278        >>> ed.bkg.InteractivePlot.Help.keyboard_shortcuts()
1279        ===============================================================
1280        '''
1281        
1282        print(Help.intelligent_dedent(help_text))
1283    
1284    
1285    def InteractivePlot_shortcuts( output_file='some_file' ):
1286        '''
1287        BGROUND printed help :: InteractivePlot :: Keyboard shortcuts
1288        '''
1289        
1290        # (1) Define output file names
1291        # (objective: all should have correct extensions
1292        # (but we want to avoid double TXT extension for the main TXT file
1293        TXTfile = output_file
1294        BKGfile = output_file + '.bp'
1295        PNGfile = output_file + '.png'
1296        if not(TXTfile.lower().endswith('.txt')): TXTfile = TXTfile + '.txt'
1297        
1298        # (2) Print help including the above defined output file names
1299        
1300        help_text = f'''
1301        ============================================================
1302        BGROUND :: Interactive plot :: Keyboard shortcuts
1303        ------------------------------------------------------------
1304        1 = add a background point (at the mouse cursor position)
1305        2 = delete a background point (close to the mouse cursor)
1306        3 = show the plot with all background points
1307        4 = show the plot with linear spline background
1308        5 = show the plot with quadratic spline background
1309        6 = show the plot with cubic spline background
1310        ------------------------------------------------------------
1311        a = background points :: load the previously saved
1312        b = background points :: save to BKG-file'
1313        (BKG-file = {BKGfile}
1314        --------
1315        s = save current plot to PNG-file:
1316        (PNG-file = {PNGfile}
1317        (note: Matplotlib UI shortcut; optional output
1318        --------
1319        t = subtract current background & save data to TXT-file
1320        (TXT-file = {TXTfile}
1321        (note: this is a universal output, applicable in all cases
1322        --------
1323        u = subtract current background & update ediff.io.Profile
1324        (ediff.io.Profile = (alternative) object with i/o data
1325        (note: the object is from ediff package; ignore if not used
1326        ------------------------------------------------------------
1327        Standard Matplotlib UI tools and shortcuts work as well.
1328        See: https://matplotlib.org/stable/users/interactive.html
1329        ============================================================
1330        '''
1331        
1332        print(Help.intelligent_dedent(help_text))
1333        
1334        
1335    def RestoreFromPoints():
1336        '''
1337        BGROUND printed help :: RestoreFromPoints
1338        '''
1339        
1340        # TODO: brief text explanation
1341        # (analogy of the same method in InterativePlot class above
1342        print("Not finished yet ...")

1370        print("TODO: Adriana ...")

Class providing simple access to help functions.

  • The help functions just print basic information on stdout.
  • The help funcs are in bground.help module and can be called from here.
>>> import bground.api as bkg
>>> bkg.Help.intro()
>>> bkg.Help.more_help()
>>> bkg.Help.InteractivePlot()
def intelligent_dedent(text: str) -> str: 
1136    def intelligent_dedent(text: str) -> str:
1137        '''
1138        Intelligent dedentation - print multi-line string reasonably.
1139
1140        Parameters
1141        ----------
1142        text : str
1143            A multi-line string to print.
1144            The initial Python indentation is ignored (Python dedentation).
1145            The internal string indentation is preserved (internal indentation).
1146
1147        Returns
1148        -------
1149        str
1150            Processed string after inteligent dedentation.
1151            
1152        Technical notes
1153        ---------------
1154        * This function is used in printed help.
1155        * The printed help messages are typically multiline texts.
1156        '''
1157        
1158        # The following algorithm created with AI/ChGPT.
1159        # The code was slightly simplified - extra commands removed.
1160        # Original comments were improved + additional comments were added.
1161        
1162        # Split {text} into lines and ignore empty leading/trailing ones
1163        # (note #1: line.strip() returns falsi if it is an empty line
1164        # (note #2: the algorithm can delete 
1165        lines = text.splitlines()
1166        # Remove possible empty line(s) at the beginning
1167        while lines and not lines[0].strip():
1168            lines.pop(0)
1169        # Remove possible empty line(s) at the end
1170        while lines and not lines[-1].strip():
1171            lines.pop()
1172        # Return empty string if the multiline string contained just empty lines
1173        if not lines:
1174            return ""
1175
1176        # Find minimum indentation (tabs or spaces) among non-empty lines.
1177        # (note #1: line.strip() returns false if it is an empty line
1178        # (note #2: re.match(r'^[\t]*',line).groupt(0) = initial tabs = \t chars
1179        # (... line with two tabs contains \t\t at the beginning = 2 chars
1180        # (... the same is done for spaces => we use r'^[ \t]*' - space is there
1181        indent_levels = [
1182            len(re.match(r'^[ \t]*', line).group(0))
1183            for line in lines if line.strip()
1184        ]
1185        min_indent = min(indent_levels)
1186
1187        # Remove {min_indent} characters from the start of each line
1188        # (this is where some unnecessary/extra commands were removed
1189        dedented = [ line[min_indent:] for line in lines ]
1190        return("\n".join(dedented))

Intelligent dedentation - print multi-line string reasonably.

Parameters
  • text (str): A multi-line string to print. The initial Python indentation is ignored (Python dedentation). The internal string indentation is preserved (internal indentation).
Returns
  • str: Processed string after inteligent dedentation.
Technical notes
  • This function is used in printed help.
  • The printed help messages are typically multiline texts.
def intro(): 
1193    def intro():
1194        '''
1195        BGROUND printed help :: Brief introduction
1196        '''
1197        
1198        help_text = '''
1199        =====================================================================
1200        BGROUND :: (semi)automated background subtraction for XY-data
1201        ---------------------------------------------------------------------
1202        * input data = XY-data (two columns)
1203            - text file or object with two (or more) columns
1204            - one of the columns = X-data, some other column = Y-data
1205            - allowed types of input (user specified input types and columns)
1206              text file, np.array or pd.DataFrame or ediff.io.Profile
1207        * output data = XY-data (four columns)
1208            - text file(s) and/or ediff.io.Profile object with four columns
1209            - cols in the text file: X, Y=Iraw, Ibkg, I=(Iraw-Ibkg)
1210            - cols in ELD = Profile: ELD.Pixels, ELD.Iraw, ELD.Ibkg, ELD.I 
1211        * semi-automated background subtraction:
1212            - computer opens an interactive plot
1213            - user defines background points with a mouse and keyboard
1214            - computer calculates the background + saves it as/when requested
1215        * fully-automated background subtraction:
1216            - user selects a method for background subtraction
1217            - computer calculates tha background and saves the output data
1218            - the output data are saved to file(s) and/or ediff.io.Profile
1219        * ediff.io.Profile
1220            - a specific type of input/output data
1221            - an object comming from our super-package ediff
1222            - the object is a 1D-profile from powder electron diffractogram 
1223        =====================================================================
1224        '''
1225        
1226        print(Help.intelligent_dedent(help_text))

BGROUND printed help :: Brief introduction

1247        >>> import ediff as ed

1257        BGROUND printed help :: InteractivePlot
1258        '''

1261        ===============================================================
1262        BGROUND :: InteractivePlot :: How it works
1263        ---------------------------------------------------------------
1264        * BGROUND opens Matplotlib interactive plot
1265        * the user defines backround points with mouse and keyboard
1266        * mouse actions/events are Matplotlib UI defaults
1267        * keyboard shortcuts/actions/events are defined by the program
1268          - keys for background definition: 1,2,3,4,5,6
1269          - keys for saving the results   : a,b,t,s,u
1270          - basic help is printed when the interactive plot opens
1271        --------------------------------------------------------------
1272        Complete help on keyboard shortcuts with detailed explanation:
1273        >>> import bground.api as bkg
1274        >>> bkg.InteractivePlot.Help.keyboard_shortcuts()

1276        Alternative access to help from EDIFF package:
1277        >>> import ediff as ed
1278        >>> ed.bkg.InteractivePlot.Help.keyboard_shortcuts()

BGROUND printed help :: InteractivePlot

def InteractivePlot_shortcuts(output_file='some_file'): 
1285    def InteractivePlot_shortcuts( output_file='some_file' ):
1286        '''
1287        BGROUND printed help :: InteractivePlot :: Keyboard shortcuts
1288        '''
1289        
1290        # (1) Define output file names
1291        # (objective: all should have correct extensions
1292        # (but we want to avoid double TXT extension for the main TXT file
1293        TXTfile = output_file
1294        BKGfile = output_file + '.bp'
1295        PNGfile = output_file + '.png'
1296        if not(TXTfile.lower().endswith('.txt')): TXTfile = TXTfile + '.txt'
1297        
1298        # (2) Print help including the above defined output file names
1299        
1300        help_text = f'''
1301        ============================================================
1302        BGROUND :: Interactive plot :: Keyboard shortcuts
1303        ------------------------------------------------------------
1304        1 = add a background point (at the mouse cursor position)
1305        2 = delete a background point (close to the mouse cursor)
1306        3 = show the plot with all background points
1307        4 = show the plot with linear spline background
1308        5 = show the plot with quadratic spline background
1309        6 = show the plot with cubic spline background
1310        ------------------------------------------------------------
1311        a = background points :: load the previously saved
1312        b = background points :: save to BKG-file'
1313        (BKG-file = {BKGfile}
1314        --------
1315        s = save current plot to PNG-file:
1316        (PNG-file = {PNGfile}
1317        (note: Matplotlib UI shortcut; optional output
1318        --------
1319        t = subtract current background & save data to TXT-file
1320        (TXT-file = {TXTfile}
1321        (note: this is a universal output, applicable in all cases
1322        --------
1323        u = subtract current background & update ediff.io.Profile
1324        (ediff.io.Profile = (alternative) object with i/o data
1325        (note: the object is from ediff package; ignore if not used
1326        ------------------------------------------------------------
1327        Standard Matplotlib UI tools and shortcuts work as well.
1328        See: https://matplotlib.org/stable/users/interactive.html
1329        ============================================================
1330        '''
1331        
1332        print(Help.intelligent_dedent(help_text))

BGROUND printed help :: InteractivePlot :: Keyboard shortcuts

class Plots: 
1373class Plots:
1374    '''
1375    Class defining plotting for all background correction methods.
1376    
1377    * The class returns Plots object.
1378    * The {Plots} object is designed as a component
1379      for arbitrary background subtraction class/method.
1380    
1381    Example
1382    
1383    >>> import bground.api as bkg
1384    >>> IN_FILE  = r'input_data.txt'
1385    >>> BKG_FILE = r'processed_data.txt'
1386    >>> SMET = bkg.Run.InteractivePlot(IN_FILE, BKG_FILE)
1387    >>> SMET.plots.plot_original()
1388    >>> SMET.plots.plot_without_bkg() 
1389    
1390    How is it done?
1391    
1392    * Using true *composition* (not just aggregation).
1393    * The component class (Plots) holds the reference to the parent class.
1394    * The parent/composite class can be any background subtraction class,
1395      such as: InteractivePlot, RestoreFromPoints, ...
1396    * The parent class (for example InteractivePlot)
1397      saves the reference to Plots during initialization
1398      by means of the following command: `self.plots = Plots(self)`. 
1399    '''
1400    
1401        
1402    def __init__(self, parent):
1403        '''
1404        Initialize {Plots} object.
1405
1406        * The {Plots} object is designed as a component
1407          for arbitrary background subtraction class/method.
1408        * The method is typically not initialized directly,
1409          but within the composite/parent class that defines
1410          a bacgkround subtraction method.
1411    
1412        Parameters
1413        ----------
1414        parent : class defining a background subtraction method
1415            An arbitrary class in this module
1416            that defines a backround subtraction method, such as:
1417            InteractivePlot, RestoreFromPoints ...
1418
1419        Returns
1420        -------
1421        Plots object
1422            The object has the only property: `self._parent = parent`.
1423            This is the whole trick that makes this class a true component.
1424        '''
1425        self._parent = parent   # aggregated object
1426
1427    
1428    @staticmethod
1429    def set_plot_params(size=(10,5), dpi=100, fontsize=8, 
1430                        my_defaults=True, my_rcParams=None):
1431        '''
1432        Set global plot parameters.
1433        
1434        * This is a static method.
1435        * Mostly for plotting in Jupyter.
1436        * It may be used internally in bkg subtraction methods,
1437          but InteractivePlot uses its own settings of global plot parameters.
1438
1439        Parameters
1440        ----------
1441        size : tuple of two floats, optional, the default is (8,6)
1442            Size of the figure (width, height) in [cm].
1443        dpi : int, optional, the defalut is 100
1444            DPI of the figure.
1445        fontsize : int, optional, the default is 8
1446            Size of the font used in figure labels etc.
1447        my_defaults : bool, optional, default is True
1448            If True, some reasonable additional defaults are set,
1449            namely line widths and formats.
1450        my_rcParams : dict, optional, default is None
1451            Dictionary in plt.rcParams format
1452            containing any other allowed global plot parameters.
1453
1454        Returns
1455        -------
1456        None
1457            The result is a modification of the global plt.rcParams variable.
1458        '''
1459        # (1) Basic arguments -----------------------------------------------------
1460        if size:  # Figure size
1461            # Convert size in [cm] to required size in [inch]
1462            size = (size[0]/2.54, size[1]/2.54)
1463            plt.rcParams.update({'figure.figsize' : size})
1464        if dpi:  # Figure dpi
1465            plt.rcParams.update({'figure.dpi' : dpi})
1466        if fontsize:  # Global font size
1467            plt.rcParams.update({'font.size' : fontsize})
1468        # (2) Additional default parameters ---------------------------------------
1469        if my_defaults:  # Default rcParams if not forbidden by my_defaults=False
1470            plt.rcParams.update({
1471                'lines.linewidth'    : 0.8,
1472                'axes.linewidth'     : 0.6,
1473                'xtick.major.width'  : 0.6,
1474                'ytick.major.width'  : 0.6,
1475                'grid.linewidth'     : 0.6,
1476                'grid.linestyle'     : ':'})
1477        # (3) Further user-defined parameter in rcParams format -------------------
1478        if my_rcParams:  # Other possible rcParams in the form of dictionary
1479            plt.rcParams.update(my_rcParams)
1480
1481
1482    def plot_original(self, title='Raw data before processing', grid=True):
1483        '''
1484        Plot raw XY-data BEFORE any processing.
1485        
1486        Parameters
1487        ----------
1488        title : str, optional
1489            Title of the plot.
1490            The default can be changed to None (no title) or any other string.
1491        grid : bool, optional, default is True
1492            If true, show grid in the plot.
1493        
1494        Returns
1495        -------
1496        None
1497            The result is the plot shown on the screen.
1498        '''        
1499        # Close all previous plots.
1500        # (necessary to avoid confusions about current plot in Jupyter
1501        plt.close('all')
1502        # Get XY-data
1503        X,Y = self._parent.data[0:2]
1504        # PLot XY-data
1505        plt.plot(X,Y, 'b-')
1506        if title is not None: plt.title(title)
1507        # ...add xy-labels and limits
1508        plt.xlabel(self._parent.pars.xlabel)
1509        plt.ylabel(self._parent.pars.ylabel)
1510        plt.xlim(self._parent.pars.xlim)
1511        plt.ylim (self._parent.pars.ylim)
1512        # ...add grid
1513        if grid is not None: plt.grid()
1514        # Show the final plot
1515        plt.tight_layout()
1516        plt.show()
1517
1518    
1519    def plot_with_bkg(       
1520            self, title='Data with background definition', grid=True):
1521        '''
1522        Plot XY-data and background AFTER the interactive plot is closed.
1523
1524        Parameters
1525        ----------
1526        title : str, optional
1527            Title of the plot.
1528            The default can be changed to None (no title) or any other string.
1529        grid : bool, optional, default is True
1530            If true, show grid in the plot.
1531
1532        Returns
1533        -------
1534        None
1535            The result is the plot shown on the screen.
1536        '''
1537        
1538        # (0) Initialize, prepare parameters for plotting
1539        # Close all previous plots.
1540        # (necessary to avoid confusions about current plot in Jupyter
1541        plt.close('all')
1542        # Verify, if the background points are defined
1543        # (some methods use bkg points, some not
1544        # (below we use and plot the bkg points only if they are defined
1545        background_points_defined = len(self._parent.background.points.X) > 0
1546        # Get XY-data
1547        X,Y = self._parent.data[0:2]
1548        # Get background points => if they are defined!
1549        if background_points_defined:
1550            Xp = self._parent.background.points.X
1551            Yp = self._parent.background.points.Y
1552        # Get background interpolation curve
1553        Xc = self._parent.background.curve.X
1554        Yc = self._parent.background.curve.Y
1555        
1556        # (1) Plot data
1557        # ... XY-data
1558        plt.plot(X,Y, 'b-')
1559        # ... background interpolation curve
1560        plt.plot(Xc,Yc, 'r--')
1561        # ... background points => if they are defined!
1562        if background_points_defined: plt.plot(Xp,Yp, 'r+')
1563        
1564        # (2) Finalize the plot
1565        # ...add title
1566        if title is not None: plt.title(title)
1567        # ...add xy-labels and limits
1568        plt.xlabel(self._parent.pars.xlabel)
1569        plt.ylabel(self._parent.pars.ylabel)
1570        plt.xlim(self._parent.pars.xlim)
1571        plt.ylim (self._parent.pars.ylim)
1572        # ...add grid
1573        if grid is not None: plt.grid()
1574        
1575        # (3) Show the finalized plot
1576        plt.tight_layout()
1577        plt.show()
1578
1579    
1580    def plot_without_bkg(
1581            self, title='Data after background subtraction', 
1582            xlim=None, ylim=None, grid=True):
1583        '''
1584        Show background-corrected XY-data AFTER the interactive plot is closed.
1585
1586        Parameters
1587        ----------
1588        title : str, optional
1589            Title of the plot.
1590            The default can be changed to None (no title) or any other string.
1591        xlim : tuple or list with two values, default is None
1592            X-axis limits [xmin,xmax].
1593            If the default value is unchanged,
1594            the limits are taken from self.ppar.xlim.
1595        ylim : tuple or list with two values, default is None
1596            Y-axis limits [ymin,ymax].
1597            If the default value is unchanged,
1598            the limits are taken from self.ppar.xlim.
1599        grid : bool, optional, default is True
1600            If the argument is True, a grid is added to the plot.
1601
1602        Returns
1603        -------
1604        None
1605            The result is the plot shown on the screen.
1606        '''
1607        
1608        # Close all previous plots.
1609        # (necessary to avoid confusions about current plot in Jupyter
1610        plt.close('all')
1611        
1612        # Re-subtract background from points => if the points exist!
1613        # (just to be sure, it is quite fast
1614        # (only for methods that use background.points
1615        if len(self._parent.background.points.X) > 0:
1616            # If there are background points defined, recalculate.
1617            # (not necessary to save result => auto-saved in self._parent.data
1618            bground.points.bfunc.calculate_bkg_data(self._parent)
1619        
1620        # Define X,Y (X-coordinate, Intensity after bkg subtraction)
1621        # (the data have been recalculated above
1622        # (OR they should be present from automated bkg subtraction methods
1623        X,Y = self._parent.data[0], self._parent.data[3]
1624        
1625        # Plot background-corrected XY-data
1626        plt.plot(X,Y, 'b-')
1627        # ...add title
1628        if title is not None:
1629            plt.title(title)
1630        # ...add xy-labels
1631        plt.xlabel(self._parent.pars.xlabel)
1632        plt.ylabel(self._parent.pars.ylabel)
1633        # ...add xy-limits
1634        if xlim is None: xlim = self._parent.pars.xlim
1635        if ylim is None: ylim = self._parent.pars.ylim
1636        plt.xlim(xlim)
1637        plt.ylim(ylim)
1638        # ...add grid
1639        if grid is True: plt.grid()
1640        # Show the final plot
1641        plt.tight_layout()
1642        plt.show()

Class defining plotting for all background correction methods.

  • The class returns Plots object.
  • The {Plots} object is designed as a component for arbitrary background subtraction class/method.

Example

>>> import bground.api as bkg
>>> IN_FILE  = r'input_data.txt'
>>> BKG_FILE = r'processed_data.txt'
>>> SMET = bkg.Run.InteractivePlot(IN_FILE, BKG_FILE)
>>> SMET.plots.plot_original()
>>> SMET.plots.plot_without_bkg()

How is it done?

  • Using true composition (not just aggregation).
  • The component class (Plots) holds the reference to the parent class.
  • The parent/composite class can be any background subtraction class, such as: InteractivePlot, RestoreFromPoints, ...
  • The parent class (for example InteractivePlot) saves the reference to Plots during initialization by means of the following command: self.plots = Plots(self).
Plots(parent) 
1402    def __init__(self, parent):
1403        '''
1404        Initialize {Plots} object.
1405
1406        * The {Plots} object is designed as a component
1407          for arbitrary background subtraction class/method.
1408        * The method is typically not initialized directly,
1409          but within the composite/parent class that defines
1410          a bacgkround subtraction method.
1411    
1412        Parameters
1413        ----------
1414        parent : class defining a background subtraction method
1415            An arbitrary class in this module
1416            that defines a backround subtraction method, such as:
1417            InteractivePlot, RestoreFromPoints ...
1418
1419        Returns
1420        -------
1421        Plots object
1422            The object has the only property: `self._parent = parent`.
1423            This is the whole trick that makes this class a true component.
1424        '''
1425        self._parent = parent   # aggregated object

Initialize {Plots} object.

  • The {Plots} object is designed as a component for arbitrary background subtraction class/method.
  • The method is typically not initialized directly, but within the composite/parent class that defines a bacgkround subtraction method.
Parameters
  • parent (class defining a background subtraction method): An arbitrary class in this module that defines a backround subtraction method, such as: InteractivePlot, RestoreFromPoints ...
Returns
  • Plots object: The object has the only property: self._parent = parent. This is the whole trick that makes this class a true component.
@staticmethod
def set_plot_params( size=(10, 5), dpi=100, fontsize=8, my_defaults=True, my_rcParams=None): 
1428    @staticmethod
1429    def set_plot_params(size=(10,5), dpi=100, fontsize=8, 
1430                        my_defaults=True, my_rcParams=None):
1431        '''
1432        Set global plot parameters.
1433        
1434        * This is a static method.
1435        * Mostly for plotting in Jupyter.
1436        * It may be used internally in bkg subtraction methods,
1437          but InteractivePlot uses its own settings of global plot parameters.
1438
1439        Parameters
1440        ----------
1441        size : tuple of two floats, optional, the default is (8,6)
1442            Size of the figure (width, height) in [cm].
1443        dpi : int, optional, the defalut is 100
1444            DPI of the figure.
1445        fontsize : int, optional, the default is 8
1446            Size of the font used in figure labels etc.
1447        my_defaults : bool, optional, default is True
1448            If True, some reasonable additional defaults are set,
1449            namely line widths and formats.
1450        my_rcParams : dict, optional, default is None
1451            Dictionary in plt.rcParams format
1452            containing any other allowed global plot parameters.
1453
1454        Returns
1455        -------
1456        None
1457            The result is a modification of the global plt.rcParams variable.
1458        '''
1459        # (1) Basic arguments -----------------------------------------------------
1460        if size:  # Figure size
1461            # Convert size in [cm] to required size in [inch]
1462            size = (size[0]/2.54, size[1]/2.54)
1463            plt.rcParams.update({'figure.figsize' : size})
1464        if dpi:  # Figure dpi
1465            plt.rcParams.update({'figure.dpi' : dpi})
1466        if fontsize:  # Global font size
1467            plt.rcParams.update({'font.size' : fontsize})
1468        # (2) Additional default parameters ---------------------------------------
1469        if my_defaults:  # Default rcParams if not forbidden by my_defaults=False
1470            plt.rcParams.update({
1471                'lines.linewidth'    : 0.8,
1472                'axes.linewidth'     : 0.6,
1473                'xtick.major.width'  : 0.6,
1474                'ytick.major.width'  : 0.6,
1475                'grid.linewidth'     : 0.6,
1476                'grid.linestyle'     : ':'})
1477        # (3) Further user-defined parameter in rcParams format -------------------
1478        if my_rcParams:  # Other possible rcParams in the form of dictionary
1479            plt.rcParams.update(my_rcParams)

Set global plot parameters.

  • This is a static method.
  • Mostly for plotting in Jupyter.
  • It may be used internally in bkg subtraction methods, but InteractivePlot uses its own settings of global plot parameters.
Parameters
  • size (tuple of two floats, optional, the default is (8,6)): Size of the figure (width, height) in [cm].
  • dpi (int, optional, the defalut is 100): DPI of the figure.
  • fontsize (int, optional, the default is 8): Size of the font used in figure labels etc.
  • my_defaults (bool, optional, default is True): If True, some reasonable additional defaults are set, namely line widths and formats.
  • my_rcParams (dict, optional, default is None): Dictionary in plt.rcParams format containing any other allowed global plot parameters.
Returns
  • None: The result is a modification of the global plt.rcParams variable.
def plot_original(self, title='Raw data before processing', grid=True): 
1482    def plot_original(self, title='Raw data before processing', grid=True):
1483        '''
1484        Plot raw XY-data BEFORE any processing.
1485        
1486        Parameters
1487        ----------
1488        title : str, optional
1489            Title of the plot.
1490            The default can be changed to None (no title) or any other string.
1491        grid : bool, optional, default is True
1492            If true, show grid in the plot.
1493        
1494        Returns
1495        -------
1496        None
1497            The result is the plot shown on the screen.
1498        '''        
1499        # Close all previous plots.
1500        # (necessary to avoid confusions about current plot in Jupyter
1501        plt.close('all')
1502        # Get XY-data
1503        X,Y = self._parent.data[0:2]
1504        # PLot XY-data
1505        plt.plot(X,Y, 'b-')
1506        if title is not None: plt.title(title)
1507        # ...add xy-labels and limits
1508        plt.xlabel(self._parent.pars.xlabel)
1509        plt.ylabel(self._parent.pars.ylabel)
1510        plt.xlim(self._parent.pars.xlim)
1511        plt.ylim (self._parent.pars.ylim)
1512        # ...add grid
1513        if grid is not None: plt.grid()
1514        # Show the final plot
1515        plt.tight_layout()
1516        plt.show()

Plot raw XY-data BEFORE any processing.

Parameters
  • title (str, optional): Title of the plot. The default can be changed to None (no title) or any other string.
  • grid (bool, optional, default is True): If true, show grid in the plot.
Returns
  • None: The result is the plot shown on the screen.
def plot_with_bkg(self, title='Data with background definition', grid=True): 
1519    def plot_with_bkg(       
1520            self, title='Data with background definition', grid=True):
1521        '''
1522        Plot XY-data and background AFTER the interactive plot is closed.
1523
1524        Parameters
1525        ----------
1526        title : str, optional
1527            Title of the plot.
1528            The default can be changed to None (no title) or any other string.
1529        grid : bool, optional, default is True
1530            If true, show grid in the plot.
1531
1532        Returns
1533        -------
1534        None
1535            The result is the plot shown on the screen.
1536        '''
1537        
1538        # (0) Initialize, prepare parameters for plotting
1539        # Close all previous plots.
1540        # (necessary to avoid confusions about current plot in Jupyter
1541        plt.close('all')
1542        # Verify, if the background points are defined
1543        # (some methods use bkg points, some not
1544        # (below we use and plot the bkg points only if they are defined
1545        background_points_defined = len(self._parent.background.points.X) > 0
1546        # Get XY-data
1547        X,Y = self._parent.data[0:2]
1548        # Get background points => if they are defined!
1549        if background_points_defined:
1550            Xp = self._parent.background.points.X
1551            Yp = self._parent.background.points.Y
1552        # Get background interpolation curve
1553        Xc = self._parent.background.curve.X
1554        Yc = self._parent.background.curve.Y
1555        
1556        # (1) Plot data
1557        # ... XY-data
1558        plt.plot(X,Y, 'b-')
1559        # ... background interpolation curve
1560        plt.plot(Xc,Yc, 'r--')
1561        # ... background points => if they are defined!
1562        if background_points_defined: plt.plot(Xp,Yp, 'r+')
1563        
1564        # (2) Finalize the plot
1565        # ...add title
1566        if title is not None: plt.title(title)
1567        # ...add xy-labels and limits
1568        plt.xlabel(self._parent.pars.xlabel)
1569        plt.ylabel(self._parent.pars.ylabel)
1570        plt.xlim(self._parent.pars.xlim)
1571        plt.ylim (self._parent.pars.ylim)
1572        # ...add grid
1573        if grid is not None: plt.grid()
1574        
1575        # (3) Show the finalized plot
1576        plt.tight_layout()
1577        plt.show()

Plot XY-data and background AFTER the interactive plot is closed.

Parameters
  • title (str, optional): Title of the plot. The default can be changed to None (no title) or any other string.
  • grid (bool, optional, default is True): If true, show grid in the plot.
Returns
  • None: The result is the plot shown on the screen.
def plot_without_bkg( self, title='Data after background subtraction', xlim=None, ylim=None, grid=True): 
1580    def plot_without_bkg(
1581            self, title='Data after background subtraction', 
1582            xlim=None, ylim=None, grid=True):
1583        '''
1584        Show background-corrected XY-data AFTER the interactive plot is closed.
1585
1586        Parameters
1587        ----------
1588        title : str, optional
1589            Title of the plot.
1590            The default can be changed to None (no title) or any other string.
1591        xlim : tuple or list with two values, default is None
1592            X-axis limits [xmin,xmax].
1593            If the default value is unchanged,
1594            the limits are taken from self.ppar.xlim.
1595        ylim : tuple or list with two values, default is None
1596            Y-axis limits [ymin,ymax].
1597            If the default value is unchanged,
1598            the limits are taken from self.ppar.xlim.
1599        grid : bool, optional, default is True
1600            If the argument is True, a grid is added to the plot.
1601
1602        Returns
1603        -------
1604        None
1605            The result is the plot shown on the screen.
1606        '''
1607        
1608        # Close all previous plots.
1609        # (necessary to avoid confusions about current plot in Jupyter
1610        plt.close('all')
1611        
1612        # Re-subtract background from points => if the points exist!
1613        # (just to be sure, it is quite fast
1614        # (only for methods that use background.points
1615        if len(self._parent.background.points.X) > 0:
1616            # If there are background points defined, recalculate.
1617            # (not necessary to save result => auto-saved in self._parent.data
1618            bground.points.bfunc.calculate_bkg_data(self._parent)
1619        
1620        # Define X,Y (X-coordinate, Intensity after bkg subtraction)
1621        # (the data have been recalculated above
1622        # (OR they should be present from automated bkg subtraction methods
1623        X,Y = self._parent.data[0], self._parent.data[3]
1624        
1625        # Plot background-corrected XY-data
1626        plt.plot(X,Y, 'b-')
1627        # ...add title
1628        if title is not None:
1629            plt.title(title)
1630        # ...add xy-labels
1631        plt.xlabel(self._parent.pars.xlabel)
1632        plt.ylabel(self._parent.pars.ylabel)
1633        # ...add xy-limits
1634        if xlim is None: xlim = self._parent.pars.xlim
1635        if ylim is None: ylim = self._parent.pars.ylim
1636        plt.xlim(xlim)
1637        plt.ylim(ylim)
1638        # ...add grid
1639        if grid is True: plt.grid()
1640        # Show the final plot
1641        plt.tight_layout()
1642        plt.show()

Show background-corrected XY-data AFTER the interactive plot is closed.

Parameters
  • title (str, optional): Title of the plot. The default can be changed to None (no title) or any other string.
  • xlim (tuple or list with two values, default is None): X-axis limits [xmin,xmax]. If the default value is unchanged, the limits are taken from self.ppar.xlim.
  • ylim (tuple or list with two values, default is None): Y-axis limits [ymin,ymax]. If the default value is unchanged, the limits are taken from self.ppar.xlim.
  • grid (bool, optional, default is True): If the argument is True, a grid is added to the plot.
Returns
  • None: The result is the plot shown on the screen.
